quotes.quote: AI gets better when you do more of it.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro surveys the emerging ecosystem of 'Dark Factories' — automated systems that turn specifications into shipping software using AI. He identifies the core pattern as the 'trycycle': a simple loop where AI writes code, checks its work, and iterates until it succeeds. He profiles three implementations of increasing complexity — Steve Yegge's Mad Max-themed Gastown, StrongDM's configurable Attractor pattern, and his own Go implementation called Kilroy — before introducing Trycycle, a minimal Claude Code skill that implements the pattern in plain English. His thesis is that AI crossed a threshold from 'slightly-lossy' to 'slightly-gainy' in iterative self-improvement, making these simple retry loops surprisingly powerful.
quotes.key_insight: The fundamental pattern powering AI software factories is a simple retry loop — plan, implement, check, repeat — that works because AI models have crossed the threshold where iterative self-correction produces net improvements.

quotes.quote: You recognize that, if you want to move quickly, you're not the person best qualified to be writing code anymore. The AI writes the code.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro describes visiting StrongDM's CTO Justin McCarthy and witnessing a 'dark factory' approach to software development where AI writes all the code and humans stop reviewing it. The core methodology fits on an index card: accept that AI writes code better and faster than you, stop being the bottleneck by reviewing every PR, recognize the terrifying problems this creates, and realize that solving those problems is now your actual job. The team built autonomous systems like 'Healer' that detect, diagnose, and fix bugs without human intervention, and created digital twins of enterprise SaaS products for realistic testing. A three-person team built the entire factory, features, and testing infrastructure, with a recent hire now training dozens of senior developers on the new approach.
quotes.key_insight: The future of software development isn't humans using AI to write code faster — it's humans building autonomous systems that write, test, and fix code without human review, making the engineer's job maintaining the factory rather than the product.

quotes.quote: If you're not writing the code, and you're still reviewing every pull request, you are the bottleneck. So you have to stop reading the code, too.

quotes.quote: If you are just using ChatGPT to write your regex, you aren't really getting the benefits of deflation. You're just typing faster.
quotes.summary: Shapiro presents a five-level framework for AI-assisted software development, modeled after the NHTSA's autonomous driving levels. The levels range from Level 0 (manual coding with occasional AI search) through Level 4 (developer becomes a PM who writes specs and checks tests after 12 hours) to Level 5, the 'Dark Factory' where a black box turns specs into software with no human involvement. He argues that most AI-native developers plateau at Level 2 (pair programming with AI) and that each level deceptively feels like the final destination. Shapiro places himself at Level 4 and notes that only a handful of small teams have reached Level 5, which he believes represents the future of software development.
quotes.key_insight: AI coding adoption follows a predictable five-level progression that systematically transforms developers from coders into managers into PMs into spectators, and most plateau far earlier than they realize.

quotes.quote: Day over day, year over year, AI is driving down the cost of writing code. Economists have a word for what happens when stuff keeps getting cheaper: deflation.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro argues that AI is creating 'technical deflation' — steadily driving down the cost of writing and fixing code. He extends Ward Cunningham's classic 'technical debt' metaphor, observing that because technical debt is owed in work rather than money, and the cost of work is deflating, carrying more debt becomes a rational strategy. Teams should ship prototypes faster and skip elegant abstractions, knowing they can repay with cheap AI hours later. Simultaneously, existing debt that causes friction has never been cheaper to eliminate. The post reframes conventional engineering wisdom: procrastination on code quality is now economically rational.
quotes.key_insight: Because AI is deflating the cost of software work, technical debt becomes a wise investment — borrow expensive human hours now and repay with cheap AI hours later.

quotes.quote: I decided to give it the ultimate challenge: I pasted in XKCD 1425, and nothing else.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro demonstrates Google AI Studio's Apps feature by giving it the ultimate test: pasting in XKCD 1425 (the famous comic about how identifying if a photo contains a bird was once a nearly impossible CS problem) with no additional instructions. The comic, published in 2014, joked that bird detection would take five years of research — a task now trivially solved by AI. Shapiro lets the results speak for themselves, implicitly making the point that AI tools have made previously impossible software tasks almost effortless. The brevity of the post is itself the argument: the feat requires so little effort it barely needs explanation.
quotes.key_insight: The fact that an AI tool can build a working app from nothing but a comic about an impossible programming task — with zero written instructions — is itself the most powerful argument for how far AI development tools have come.

quotes.quote: My wife pointed out that most of my shirts predate the pandemic, and it was time to shop for something new.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his attempt to use generative AI tools as a personal fashion consultant after his wife suggested he needed new clothes. He tested multiple image generators to visualize startup CEO outfits, then used Sora to create a video of himself in various looks, which actually produced promising results. When he tried to go further by using ChatGPT's agent mode to autonomously shop for clothes and generate a Sora video, the system failed after thirty minutes of effort. The post serves as a lighthearted illustration of where AI capabilities currently stand — impressive at generation, but not yet ready for multi-step autonomous tasks.
quotes.key_insight: Current AI excels at creative generation tasks like styling and visualization but breaks down when asked to autonomously chain together multiple real-world actions.

He tested multiple image generators to visualize startup CEO outfits, then used Sora to create a video of himself in various looks, which actually produced promising results. When he tried to go further by using ChatGPT's agent mode to autonomously shop for clothes and generate a Sora video, the system failed after thirty minutes of effort. The post serves as a lighthearted illustration of where AI capabilities currently stand — impressive at generation, but not yet ready for multi-step autonomous tasks.
quotes.key_insight: Current AI excels at creative generation tasks like styling and visualization but breaks down when asked to autonomously chain together multiple real-world actions.

quotes.quote: I didn't read the compatibility notes. I didn't check to see if it had Windows support. I needed coffee.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts how he casually asked Codex CLI to install Steve Yegge's Beads tool on Windows, only to discover it had no Windows support—and Codex autonomously ported it instead. He contrasts this with a 2023 BabyAGI disaster where an agent burned $122 in API calls trying to compile GCC from source just to recommend a standing desk. The juxtaposition illustrates how far AI agents have come in two years: from expensive recursive spirals to genuinely useful autonomous problem-solving. He notes Codex CLI's advantage of running on a flat subscription (no surprise bills) and its ability to stay on task, while acknowledging Claude Code has better tooling but GPT-5-codex is more focused.
quotes.key_insight: The gap between AI agents that spiral into expensive absurdity and ones that autonomously solve real problems has closed in just two years, turning accidental software ports into casual Tuesday mornings.

quotes.quote: I did not get a standing desk recommendation. I got a $122 lesson in why you don't let agents recurse unsupervised.

quotes.quote: You can delegate all your work in parallel and pick the winner.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro describes a paradigm shift in AI-assisted development he calls 'slot machine development': dispatching multiple parallel AI coding requests, having each instance test its own work, and picking the best result. He learned the approach from StrongDM CTO Justin McCarthy and overcame his initial skepticism that every AI checkin must be manually reviewed. The method works because AI can now judge its own output, follow directions reliably, and the cost per token is trivial compared to developer-hours. Shapiro frames this as the next step in scaling compute: from training, to serial reasoning tokens, to parallel inference tokens.
quotes.key_insight: The next frontier in AI-assisted development is not better single-thread prompting but massively parallel generation with automated self-evaluation — brute-forcing quality through volume and judgment.

quotes.quote: Slot-machine development is just N versions in parallel, a judge, and a budget.

quotes.quote: Don't invest too much time in making the state of the art perform better. The state of the art will perform better on its own.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro revisits a set of ten AI principles he wrote in June 2023, just six months after ChatGPT launched, and evaluates how well they held up over two years. His rules cover model improvement velocity, provider lock-in risks, hosted vs. self-hosted models, multimodality, and the critical importance of validated data. He finds most predictions proved accurate, with minor adjustments on fine-tuning and context windows. The overarching message is that despite AI's breakneck pace of change, certain strategic principles about avoiding lock-in, staying current, and valuing ground truth remain durable.
quotes.key_insight: Despite AI's rapid evolution, the most durable strategic principles are about what to avoid — lock-in, long-term bets, and trusting unvalidated data — rather than what specific technology to adopt.

quotes.quote: Startups keep springing up whose core benefit is 'prompt/finetune AI to make it better at ___'. They keep getting eaten by the models just getting better all by themselves.

quotes.quote: Every time I travel to a city, I wrack my brain to try to remember who lives there so I can reach out to visit. And I'm terrible at it.
quotes.summary: Shapiro shares a quick productivity hack for travelers who struggle to remember contacts in cities they're visiting. By downloading your Google Calendar via Takeout and uploading it to ChatGPT with o3, you can generate a spreadsheet of everyone you've met with in a specific area. The post demonstrates how AI can solve a small but persistent personal annoyance with minimal effort. It's a concise, practical example of using LLMs for ad-hoc data wrangling tasks.
quotes.key_insight: AI chatbots can serve as instant, no-code data analysts for messy personal data tasks that would otherwise require scripting or simply go unsolved.

quotes.quote: That's it. Sit back and watch the gears whirl for a bit, then download a spreadsheet of all the folks you're overdue to catch up with.

quotes.quote: I'm working with them to share some of the the lessons we've learned building generative AI tools at Glowforge so more people can put them to work.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he has joined Wharton's Generative AI Lab as a research fellow, working alongside Ethan and Lilach Mollick. He plans to share lessons learned from building generative AI tools at Glowforge and investigate unusual phenomena he's observed in LLMs. He emphasizes this is a side project alongside his continued role as Glowforge CEO. The post is brief and serves mainly as a teaser for upcoming content.
quotes.key_insight: Shapiro is bridging the gap between hands-on AI product development at Glowforge and academic research at Wharton, positioning himself to formalize and share observations about LLM behavior.

quotes.quote: Talking about race and gender is deeply uncomfortable for me. It's probably uncomfortable for you too. But that's OK – we're here to grow, and to learn, and we'll stumble through it together.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a five-minute talk he gave at ORD Camp, an unconference, addressed to fellow white men about recognizing their default behaviors around speaking and taking up space. He argues that white men are socialized to speak and teach even when they're not the most knowledgeable, and that this crowds out other voices. He offers three practical tools: plan which sessions to lead vs. listen in, take notes to resist the urge to speak, and mentally roll a d20 before talking (only speak on a natural 20). He illustrates the problem with an anecdote about white men dominating a session on underrepresentation in tech, and frames the advice as making the event better for everyone.
quotes.key_insight: Good intentions about making space for others require concrete behavioral tools — like the d20 rule — because socialized defaults are too strong to override with awareness alone.

quotes.quote: A common white dude default, certainly my default, is speaking/teaching – even when, perhaps, we're not the world's greatest authority on the topic.

quotes.quote: I missed my chance to save the life of another human being because I didn't check the mail.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares that six years after missing a chance to donate bone marrow due to inbox neglect, he has been matched again as a potential donor. He reflects on the deep regret of his earlier failure to respond and expresses gratitude for a second opportunity. The post serves as both a personal update and a call to action for readers to sign up as marrow donors.
quotes.key_insight: Small acts of negligence like ignoring your inbox can have life-or-death consequences, but sometimes you get a rare second chance to make it right.

quotes.quote: I've never forgotten that, and it's one of my deepest regrets.

quotes.quote: "Angel investor" is a stupid and meaningless term, right up there with "stock market investor" or "bank account holder". It means someone did something, once. It means nothing more.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro confesses that he's become the kind of angel investor he once found frustrating as a founder. He describes his investment strategy as actively trying to avoid investing, writing tiny $10k checks, doing minimal due diligence by piggybacking on trusted lead investors, and providing almost no ongoing involvement. The post is a self-deprecating guide to why founders probably shouldn't want him as an investor, while revealing honest truths about how many small angel investors actually operate. He acknowledges he only invests when the CEO is a friend, the company is already familiar, and top-tier investors are already involved.
quotes.key_insight: Most small angel investors add minimal value and maximum hassle, and the honest ones will tell you so themselves.

quotes.quote: My investment strategy is to try and avoid investing in startups. I don't look for deals; I actively try to avoid them.

quotes.quote: I had to reconcile some fundamental tensions. Elissa had a lot of respect for her cofounder based on firsthand interaction, but I, based on a small amount of second hand information, did not.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro discusses a chapter from his book 'Hot Seat' that tells the story of Elissa Shevinsky, CEO of Glimpse, and the controversy surrounding her cofounder's offensive tweets that made tech headlines. Shapiro reflects on the difficulty of telling someone else's story while maintaining his own editorial integrity, especially when his views diverged from his subject's more generous interpretation. He notes that Shevinsky showed remarkable grace by refusing to fully blame her cofounder despite the turmoil his actions caused. The post also explains that his publishers required him to remove the actual tweets from the book, so he provides links to the coverage for context.
quotes.key_insight: Telling startup stories honestly requires navigating the tension between respecting your subject's perspective and maintaining your own editorial integrity, especially when the story touches on the tech industry's cultural failures.

quotes.quote: This was Elissa's story, but I am the one telling it. I needed to accurately reflect what she told me, but I needed to be sure I believed what I was writing.

quotes.quote: We're making a real, tangible thing – a piece of hardware – powered by a giant stack of software.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces the formation of Glowforge, a new hardware startup building a product he describes as approaching science fiction. The company is creating a hardware device powered by a large software stack that enables people to create real, beautiful products simply. He highlights the experienced team he's assembled, including Y Combinator alumni and seasoned engineers. The post serves as both an announcement and a recruiting call for engineers to join the founding team.
quotes.key_insight: Glowforge aims to democratize physical product creation by building a software-powered hardware device that makes manufacturing as simple as possible for everyday people.

quotes.quote: I worked hard on the Robot Turtles campaign, but fundamentally thought of it as a fun diversion. I had no idea it was going to turn into the blockbuster that it did!
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro compiles his best resources on running a successful Kickstarter campaign, drawn from his experience with Robot Turtles — the most-backed board game in Kickstarter history, raising over $631K. The post serves as a curated guide linking to his detailed writings and interviews covering campaign construction, video strategy, shipping logistics, production, and timing. He frames the Robot Turtles campaign as an unexpected blockbuster that started as a fun diversion. The collection spans mid-campaign data analysis, post-campaign video breakdowns, logistics deep-dives, and podcast interviews, offering a comprehensive playbook for aspiring Kickstarter creators.
quotes.key_insight: A successful Kickstarter campaign requires deliberate strategy across video, structure, timing, and logistics — and sharing those lessons transparently helps the entire creator community.

quotes.quote: I have seen this perspective before: 'If we ignore the problem, it will go away'. I think it would work for sexism as well as it does for Ebola.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro responds to Microsoft Ventures head Rahul Sood's tweet calling Startup Weekend Women 'pointless.' Shapiro dismantles Sood's argument that drawing attention to gender separation perpetuates it, comparing this logic to ignoring Ebola and likening it to a child's lack of object permanence. He presents testimony from women entrepreneurs who credit women-specific events with launching their careers. The post argues that women-focused startup events are essential for widening the entrepreneurship funnel and that dismissing them dishonors the volunteers and organizers who make them possible.
quotes.key_insight: Women-specific startup events are not redundant but essential pipeline-builders, and dismissing them as pointless reflects a naive belief that ignoring systemic problems makes them disappear.

quotes.quote: A lightweight, insanely powerful plane that can fold flat in your suitcase and shoot screaming aerial videos for $99.78.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro provides a detailed parts list and guide for building a high-performance RC plane for under $100. The post accompanies his appearance on the Gweek podcast with Mark Frauenfelder, where they discussed Robot Turtles, maker culture, and RC flying. Shapiro walks through every component needed — from the Turnigy Bonsai airframe to motors, props, batteries, servos, and a keychain camera — with prices and practical tips. He also touches on more advanced setups like FPV rigs and long-endurance UAVs, and shares aerial footage from flights over Maui.
quotes.key_insight: With under $100 in commodity hobby parts and some basic soldering skills, anyone can build a high-performance RC plane capable of shooting aerial video.

quotes.quote: If you already have the app, and are just now starting to think about marketing, you're probably hosed.
quotes.summary: Shapiro argues that startups should figure out their marketing before building their product. Inspired by Amazon's practice of writing press releases before development, he proposes testing demand through low-cost marketing artifacts like Kickstarter videos, mock app store pages, and blog posts. The marketing that resonates becomes a north star for product development, clarifying which features matter most. He admits he hasn't tried this approach himself yet but plans to.
quotes.key_insight: Marketing isn't just how you sell your product — it's how you discover what your product should be, so do it before you build anything.

quotes.quote: You get to convey at most two ideas: one that you say, and one that you show.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro breaks down the strategy behind the Kickstarter video for Robot Turtles, the bestselling board game in Kickstarter history at the time, which raised $631,230. He explains that an effective Kickstarter video should convey at most two ideas—one spoken and one visual—and describes how he split his message: the talk track covered programming education while the visuals hammered home 'Happy Family' imagery. The video was produced under extreme time pressure, with a hastily organized playtest doubling as a video shoot using borrowed cameras and children of startup friends. Shapiro emphasizes that authentic, unscripted footage outperformed rehearsed takes, achieving an implied 20% conversion rate from video views to pledges.
quotes.key_insight: The most effective Kickstarter videos pair a single clear spoken message with a single repeated visual emotion, and authentic imperfection converts better than polished scripting.

quotes.quote: It's worth the money to hire a pro, but let reality shine through.

quotes.quote: Marketing and complexity are a bad combination.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro breaks down the strategy behind the Kickstarter video for Robot Turtles, the bestselling board game in Kickstarter history at the time, which raised $631,230. He explains that an effective Kickstarter video should convey at most two ideas—one spoken and one visual—and describes how he split his message: the talk track covered programming education while the visuals hammered home 'Happy Family' imagery. The video was produced under extreme time pressure, with a hastily organized playtest doubling as a video shoot using borrowed cameras and children of startup friends. Shapiro emphasizes that authentic, unscripted footage outperformed rehearsed takes, achieving an implied 20% conversion rate from video views to pledges.
quotes.key_insight: The most effective Kickstarter videos pair a single clear spoken message with a single repeated visual emotion, and authentic imperfection converts better than polished scripting.

quotes.quote: I find there are roughly two types of people on Kickstarter: the givers, who are backing projects because they believe in them, and for whom the reward is secondary — and the shoppers, who are looking for a deal on something cool.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a mid-campaign retrospective on his Robot Turtles Kickstarter, which raised $250k in 12 days after being funded in just 5 hours. He breaks down the key decisions that drove success: crafting a compelling hook ('board game that teaches programming to preschoolers'), setting an achievable funding goal of $25k, pricing for shoppers rather than donors, and leveraging personal networks over press for initial momentum. The post includes detailed analytics on backer sources, conversion rates, and funding curves, revealing that Kickstarter's real power is its funnel psychology — 20% of video viewers pledged. He concludes that most traffic comes from off-site sources and that even top campaigns rarely exceed 10,000 backers.
quotes.key_insight: Crowdfunding success comes from combining a counterintuitive hook that generates organic buzz, a low enough goal to create early momentum, and pricing that treats backers as shoppers — not patrons.

quotes.quote: I'm humbled and amazed by the response – as of now, six days in, it's at $188,000, or about seven times what I'd hoped to raise.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces the Kickstarter launch of Robot Turtles, a board game designed to teach preschoolers programming concepts. Six days into the campaign, it had raised $188,000—about seven times his original goal. He emphasizes the game as a way for adults to meaningfully interact with kids through play. The post is essentially a call to action for readers to back the project and share it on social media before the print deadline of September 27.
quotes.key_insight: A board game teaching preschoolers to program struck a massive nerve, raising 7x its Kickstarter goal in under a week largely through organic word-of-mouth sharing.

quotes.quote: I want to get this in the hands of as many eager grownups as I can, because having played it, it's just an amazing way to interact with kids.

quotes.quote: Suggesting improvements is like optimizations to their shampooing technique… it's just not really a subject of life that's much open to change.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his journey to replicate the intensely strong cold brew coffee he discovered at a Google office, made from an undiluted Yama cold brew drip maker he dubs the 'Breaking Bad Percolator.' After experimenting with mason jars, french presses, and a discontinued Bodum iced coffee maker, he settles on the humble Toddy as the best practical solution. The post is a lighthearted product-review-meets-iteration-story, documenting each failed attempt and the lessons learned. His conclusion: skip the middle options and go with either the cheapest (Toddy) or most expensive (Yama) cold brew method, and always use quality beans.
quotes.key_insight: When iterating on a solution, the cheapest option that works and the premium option are both worth buying, but the mediocre middle ground is a waste of time.

quotes.quote: Drinking cold-brew coffee without diluting it first is AWESOME, more or less akin to putting four shots of espresso on ice in intensity.

quotes.quote: And all the rumors are true: Google is truly a magical place to be.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he's taking a leave of absence from Google, where he'd spent two years after Google acquired his startup Sparkbuy. He praises Google's culture, food, people, and internal accountability, calling it a genuinely magical place to work. He's leaving temporarily to finish a book about startup CEOs for O'Reilly Publishing and to work on a mysterious non-software product. The post serves as both a love letter to Google and a teaser for his next entrepreneurial chapter.
quotes.key_insight: Even when working at the best big company in tech, the entrepreneurial itch to build something new eventually wins out.

quotes.quote: Because hardware's hard, and hardware's different.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces his advisory role at Highway One, a San Francisco-based hardware incubator backed by PCH, one of the world's largest manufacturing companies. He argues that hardware startups need specialized support because hardware is fundamentally different from software. Highway One's unique value proposition is direct access to PCH's manufacturing expertise and factories in Shenzhen, which would otherwise be inaccessible to startups. The program offers hands-on manufacturing experience, engineering talent, and a $20k investment for 3%-6% equity.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest barrier for hardware startups isn't funding or ideas but access to world-class manufacturing expertise, which purpose-built incubators backed by major manufacturers can uniquely provide.

quotes.quote: We spent a lot of time figuring out our location, because that would be hard to change later. We didn't worry much about our precise campsite until we got there and wandered around a bit.
quotes.summary: Shapiro draws a playful extended analogy between family camping and launching a startup. Each observation about the camping trip — choosing a location carefully, seeking contradictory advice, over-preparing, procrastinating out of fear — maps directly onto the startup experience. The post's punchline reveals the dual meaning, ending with a recommendation for both camping and entrepreneurship. It's a lighthearted reminder that startups, like camping, are messy, uncomfortable, and ultimately rewarding.
quotes.key_insight: Starting a company, like camping for the first time, is scary and messy — but the discomfort is worth it, and no amount of preparation substitutes for just doing it.

quotes.quote: If someone makes you an offer, it's because they're interested. If they're interested, you should negotiate. Not accept; negotiate.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro analyzes the three worst negotiation mistakes entrepreneurs make on ABC's Shark Tank. He argues that contestants consistently fail to negotiate offers, arrive unprepared for common deal structures, and make the critical error of leaving the room when asked. Drawing from negotiation fundamentals, he explains that sharks deliberately trash businesses before offering to soften entrepreneurs into accepting bad terms. He provides tactical advice for each scenario, including how to handle counteroffers, royalties, contingencies, and colluding investors. The post concludes that real-world fundraising mirrors the show, and entrepreneurs should study the sharks' tactics rather than emulate the contestants.

quotes.quote: Dream of striking Kickstarter gold, but lack inspiration? I'm here to help.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro proposes five product ideas he'd love to see funded on Kickstarter, ranging from customizable notebooks and tablet-powered laser shows to combination RC vehicles, machine-washable dress shirts, and a sleek key consolidator. Each idea is presented with specific bonus features and price points, reflecting a maker's eye for practical innovation. The post closes with a self-aware joke about the glut of 3D printers on Kickstarter. Overall, it's a lighthearted wish list that reveals Shapiro's product sensibility—favoring practical, well-designed physical goods over hype.
quotes.key_insight: The best Kickstarter opportunities come from everyday frustrations that existing products inexplicably fail to solve, not from chasing the latest tech trend.

quotes.quote: While the explicit message he told me was about sales strategy, there was a very strong implicit message: 'I'm judging my staff by something other than how effective they are'.
quotes.summary: Shapiro recounts a coffee meeting with a promising startup CEO who casually said he needed 'someone with a sexy voice' for cold-calling. Rather than letting it slide, Shapiro agreed with the underlying sales strategy but pushed back on the language, explaining it signals judging staff on criteria other than effectiveness. The CEO was receptive, later emailing that he hadn't realized how his language might alienate candidates. Shapiro frames this as a lesson in how casual sexist language in startups is counterproductive, legally risky, and filters for the wrong people.
quotes.key_insight: Casual language around hiring reveals implicit values — and when those values prioritize attractiveness over effectiveness, they repel good candidates, invite legal risk, and undermine the actual business goal.

quotes.quote: The goal of the title is not to inform, or promote, or educate: it's to get the perfect person to click on it.
quotes.summary: Shapiro reviews three job postings from Hacker News's YC-exclusive jobs board, evaluating how well each uses its limited headline real estate to attract the right candidates. He finds that most postings waste their space on company names and generic job titles, while the best ones prioritize what makes the role compelling. The post argues that a job listing headline's purpose is not to inform but to get the perfect person to click. He scores the 22 active postings and finds only 7 attempted anything beyond a bare company-name-plus-title format.
quotes.key_insight: When you have limited space to attract talent, cut your company name and lead with what makes the role irresistible — the right people will click to find out who you are.

quotes.quote: Given limited real estate, they prioritized wisely. They tell the reader what kind of person will be a good fit. They explain why the job is going to be different and interesting.

quotes.quote: I think Paul may be correct, but he misses Chris's genius.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro analyzes Chris Zacharias's unconventional fundraising strategy of pricing his round below market rate to attract value-conscious angel investors. Paul Graham challenged this approach, arguing angel investors shouldn't be price-sensitive and that the evidence was anecdotal. Shapiro argues that regardless of whether Graham is technically correct, Zacharias is employing a brilliant 'contrarian segmentation' strategy. By fishing in a smaller, less competitive pool of investors, Zacharias gets faster access to the best investors in that segment, even if he pays some additional cost for the unconventional approach.
quotes.key_insight: Deliberately targeting an underserved, less competitive market segment — even one that seems irrational — can be a winning strategy because having no competition outweighs the costs of the unconventional approach.

quotes.quote: Believe me when I say that if you give me a good idea, I am obligated by my responsibilities to my employer to make use of it.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro explains why he deliberately avoided learning about a Techstars startup's plans because they operated in a space overlapping with his day job. Despite generally advocating against excessive confidentiality concerns, he recognized this situation was different — his fiduciary obligations to his employer meant any good ideas shared with him could be used competitively. He shares his candid email exchange with the startup founder to illustrate the nuance between personal trustworthiness and structural conflicts of interest. The core message is that trust alone isn't sufficient protection when someone's obligations run counter to your interests.
quotes.key_insight: Trustworthiness and safety are not the same thing — a person can be entirely honest and still be structurally obligated to act against your interests.

quotes.quote: Clever, well meaning, adorable, and more clueless than a general with a gmail account.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro identifies three common mistakes Kickstarter creators make that drive away potential backers: dodging important product details, rushing out stretch goals without proper planning, and downplaying risks. Using a men's clothing project as a case study, he argues that transparency and honesty matter more than polish. He contends that creators should embrace polarizing decisions and be upfront about limitations rather than trying to appeal to everyone. The post ends on a positive note, with the example company overhauling their page in response to feedback.
quotes.key_insight: Kickstarter backers are taking a bet on your team, and the fastest way to lose that bet is substituting marketing spin for honest transparency about your product, plans, and risks.

He contends that creators should embrace polarizing decisions and be upfront about limitations rather than trying to appeal to everyone. The post ends on a positive note, with the example company overhauling their page in response to feedback.
quotes.key_insight: Kickstarter backers are taking a bet on your team, and the fastest way to lose that bet is substituting marketing spin for honest transparency about your product, plans, and risks.

quotes.quote: Pro tip: if I, who know your business as well as I know the feeding habits of the Springbok Antelope, can come up with more risks than you can, you're not doing it right.

quotes.quote: I'm running a live, world-writable spreadsheet of my notes on the pitches from Techstars demo day 2012.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a live, world-writable Google Spreadsheet where he's taking notes on startup pitches from Techstars Demo Day 2012. He invites readers to follow along in real-time and contribute questions and comments. This is a repeat of a format he previously used for Y Combinator Demo Day.
quotes.key_insight: A brief logistical post inviting readers to follow along with live collaborative notes on Techstars Demo Day 2012 pitches.

quotes.quote: There is no love like a first time entrepreneur's love with nondisclosure agreements.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro demystifies NDAs by explaining that they rarely serve their intended purpose of preventing information leaks. He argues that NDAs are practically unenforceable in court because it's nearly impossible to prove someone leaked a specific secret. Instead, NDAs function primarily as tools for legal intimidation—a 'force multiplier in legal blustering.' The key dynamic governing whether NDAs get signed is leverage: whoever has the money or power can demand signatures. Shapiro advises entrepreneurs to stop asking investors to sign NDAs (it signals inexperience) and instead reserve NDA requests for situations where you hold the leverage.
quotes.key_insight: NDAs are not confidentiality tools but leverage instruments—they get signed when one party has power over the other, and asking for one when you lack leverage only signals inexperience.

quotes.quote: But that's a giant stinking load of donkey dung. It's almost never going to happen that you actually sue someone for disclosing a secret and prevail.

quotes.quote: I just maxed out one of my credit cards. I should get a badge for that.
quotes.summary: Shapiro uses the conceit of a social game called 'StartupVille' to catalog the shared milestones and indignities of startup life. Born from a late-night conversation with a fellow founder who had just maxed out a credit card, the post lists satirical badges founders can earn for experiences like site outages, awkward CEO conversations, and cleaning the office toilet. The format gamifies the unglamorous reality behind startup mythology. The post closes with a frank acknowledgment that the startup game isn't for everyone, and some players end up broke and begging for a corporate job.
quotes.key_insight: The shared indignities and financial risks of startup life are so universal they can be reduced to game badges, which says something both funny and unsettling about the culture.

quotes.quote: A note with a cap does not imply that the company's value is equal to the cap.
quotes.summary: Shapiro corrects a common misconception in startup financing: that a convertible note's cap equals the company's valuation. He recounts a conversation where founders conflated YC companies' convertible debt caps of $10-20M with actual valuations. He explains that convertible debt is a loan that converts to equity at either the next investor's price or the cap, whichever is lower. Using a reductio ad absurdum argument—uncapped notes would imply infinite valuation—he demonstrates that a cap merely sets an upper bound, not a value.
quotes.key_insight: A convertible note's cap sets a ceiling on conversion price, not a valuation—conflating the two inflates perceived startup valuations and reflects a fundamental misunderstanding of the instrument.

quotes.quote: If the cap is the valuation, then those companies are infinitely valuable, since the cap is infinite. Which is, of course, absurd.

quotes.quote: If two people work on a task, it takes twice as long.
quotes.summary: Shapiro recounts how he and his Ontela co-founders confused individual empowerment with collective decision-making, inadvertently creating the same bureaucratic slowness they'd left big companies to escape. By involving all founders in every decision, they consumed two or three people's time for marginal improvement in decision quality. Disagreements compounded the problem, making collaboration even slower. The fix was learning to trust each founder to own their domain and make final calls independently. The post argues that startups win through execution speed, not decision perfection, and that trust is the ingredient that lets small teams move fast.
quotes.key_insight: Startups lose more from slow, committee-driven decisions than they gain from marginally better ones — trust each founder to own their domain and move fast independently.

quotes.quote: The potent entrepreneur's life is full of inflection moments: brief opportunities to shift the destiny of your company.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro introduces the 'Pyramid Pitch' framework, arguing that entrepreneurs should structure their pitches like Maslow's hierarchy of needs — addressing the most fundamental questions first and building upward. Using his startup Ontela as an example, he demonstrates how each layer of the pitch answers the listener's most pressing question at that moment, from 'what do you do?' to 'how do you make money?' The key principle is that if the conversation stops at any point, the listener walks away with a coherent understanding. He contrasts this with three common anti-patterns: the story pitch, the business plan pitch, and the feature pitch, all of which bury the lede.
quotes.key_insight: The best pitches are structured like a pyramid — each layer answers the listener's most urgent question, so the conversation remains coherent and compelling no matter when it gets interrupted.

quotes.quote: Flying autonomous deliverybots are coming. Fast.
quotes.summary: Shapiro evaluates three aircraft designs—traditional helicopters, airplanes, and multicopters—for autonomous drone delivery of food (framed around the viral 'Tacocopter' concept). He systematically analyzes each design's strengths and weaknesses across lift capacity, flight efficiency, maneuverability, redundancy, and safety. While airplanes win on range and helicopters on agility, multicopters emerge as the clear winner due to their mechanical simplicity, cheap redundancy, lower prop mass, and ability to support blade guards—all critical for safe urban delivery.
quotes.key_insight: The winning drone delivery design isn't the one with the best performance on any single axis, but the one that's simplest, most redundant, and least likely to hurt someone when something goes wrong.

quotes.quote: Helicopters are basically a giant spinning ball of disaster continuously waiting to happen, and when anything goes wrong, it does.

He systematically analyzes each design's strengths and weaknesses across lift capacity, flight efficiency, maneuverability, redundancy, and safety. While airplanes win on range and helicopters on agility, multicopters emerge as the clear winner due to their mechanical simplicity, cheap redundancy, lower prop mass, and ability to support blade guards—all critical for safe urban delivery.
quotes.key_insight: The winning drone delivery design isn't the one with the best performance on any single axis, but the one that's simplest, most redundant, and least likely to hurt someone when something goes wrong.

quotes.quote: Multi-taco-copters outperform heli-taco-copters in the crucial tiebreaker of 'not killing people with spinning death from above'.

quotes.quote: With that many companies to keep track of, it helps to take notes!
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his experience attending YCombinator Demo Day, where over 70 companies presented. He took notes in a public Google Docs spreadsheet, which unexpectedly became a collaborative live document. A few hundred people joined in real-time, including YC partners and the presenting companies themselves, adding questions and commentary. The post is a brief share of the spreadsheet link for those interested in the YC batch.
quotes.key_insight: A simple shared spreadsheet can turn a one-directional demo day into a collaborative, community-driven experience.

quotes.quote: A few hundred (at least) people joined in, and there were questions and additions from all over (including some of the YC partners and the companies themselves).

quotes.quote: I didn't actually grok what was going on until I sat on the other side, looking at investments.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro explains why investors refuse to meet with companies that compete with their existing portfolio investments. Drawing from his experience on both sides of the fundraising table, he dismantles three common entrepreneur objections: that the companies aren't really competing, that an initial conversation would clarify things, and that the entrepreneur doesn't mind shared investors. He then reveals the deeper reason: even without any wrongdoing, an investor who meets with competitors risks reputation damage and legal exposure when portfolio companies inevitably converge on similar ideas.
quotes.key_insight: Investors avoid competitors not out of closed-mindedness but because even innocent exposure to competing pitches creates unavoidable reputational and legal risk when companies inevitably converge on similar ideas.

quotes.quote: Telling an investor that there's no conflict is like telling them what they should eat for dinner – no matter how much you know about the situation, they know more.

quotes.quote: I've always known that the greatest way to help startups is by building a great startup.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro reflects on seven years of helping startups and distills his approach into actionable principles. He argues the best way to help the ecosystem is by building a great startup yourself, but for those giving advice, efficiency and directness matter most. He advocates for batch mentoring, prioritizing companies with traction, strict time management, and staying in your lane of expertise. The post culminates in a new policy: he'll ask startups seeking his advice to let him write about their situations for an O'Reilly book, turning individual help into collective wisdom.
quotes.key_insight: The most scalable way to help startups is to convert individual mentoring conversations into shared knowledge, turning private advice into a public good.

quotes.quote: Dan, we're a startup. Of course we're for sale. This fucking plant on my desk here is for sale, if the price is right. What do you think we're doing here?
quotes.summary: Shapiro recounts a formative conversation with his CEO at Wildseed, a quirky Linux-based handset startup in 2002. When Shapiro confronted CEO Eric Engstrom about rumors the company was for sale, Engstrom's blunt response — that of course the company is for sale, because that's what startups do — reshaped his understanding of startup economics. Shapiro contrasts this pragmatic view with a friend who would refuse anything less than a multibillion-dollar IPO. The post argues that for most startup founders, being open to acquisition is simply rational, not a failure of ambition.
quotes.key_insight: For most startup founders, the company being 'for sale' isn't a betrayal of mission — it's the entire point of the venture.

quotes.quote: He told me – and I quite believe him – that he would sell the company for a billion dollars over his dead body.

quotes.quote: What if every utterance contained within a vow of monastic silence, digitally enforced?
quotes.summary: Shapiro describes his experiment creating a mailing list where each subscriber can only post once per year. Frustrated by the universal decay of mailing list quality — monopolized conversations, flame wars, and topic drift — he explored game mechanics for self-regulation. After nearly reinventing Hacker News with a complex voting system, he stripped the idea down to its most extreme element: a one-year enforced cool-down after every post. The result, 'The Best Thing This Year,' attracted nearly 2,000 subscribers but very few submissions, proving that artificial scarcity of voice dramatically increases both quality and silence.
quotes.key_insight: When you make every utterance costly, most people choose silence — and the few who speak produce something worth hearing.

quotes.quote: Stock grants are, at their heart, a crude hack to avoid taxes. Vesting is a hack to the hack – and one almost every founder needs.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro explains that stock vesting is fundamentally a tax hack layered on top of another tax hack, but one that founders desperately need. Using a hypothetical where a departing co-founder walks away with equal equity despite contributing nothing, he illustrates why vesting protects committed founders. He argues that founder vesting is actually founder-friendly—the opposite of common perception—and should be paired with acceleration clauses to prevent losing value during acquisitions. The post provides detailed practical advice on vesting schedules, cliff periods, acceleration triggers, and 83(b) elections.
quotes.key_insight: Founder vesting, despite its reputation as an investor demand, is actually a founder-protective mechanism that should be adopted proactively at incorporation and paired with acceleration clauses.

quotes.quote: Lightsail makes regenerative brakes for the power grid.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro profiles Lightsail Energy, a startup founded by 25-year-old middle school dropout Danielle Fong that builds regenerative braking systems for the power grid. The post playfully builds suspense through a series of reveals: the young founder, the compelling energy storage technology, and their Ghostbusters-style firehouse headquarters. Shapiro draws an analogy between how a Prius recaptures braking energy and how Lightsail helps power plants and renewable energy sources store and release electricity on demand. The post is structured as an escalating series of 'that's not the amazing part' beats, culminating in the reveal that they moved from a Ghostbusters firehouse to a chocolate factory.
quotes.key_insight: The best startups combine serious technology with irrepressible personality, and Lightsail Energy — with its young prodigy founder, grid-scale energy storage tech, and Ghostbusters firehouse headquarters — embodies that combination.

quotes.quote: You know how your Prius takes the power from the brakes when you don't need it, and dumps it back in to the engine when you do need it? Lightsail does the same thing for energy plants that can't scale their production quickly with the needs of consumers.

quotes.quote: If we keep this bullshit up, we're going to crap all over another generation of women tech entrepreneurs.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro calls out casual sexism in the startup and tech community, recounting an incident where a well-credentialed female panel moderator was introduced by referencing her appearance and marital status rather than her professional accomplishments. He cites additional examples of objectifying behavior he's witnessed, including a Techstars team using gratuitous photos of women in a pitch deck and a founder commenting on a receptionist's appearance. He argues that tolerating this behavior will drive away women tech entrepreneurs and urges people to speak up when they see it, while admitting he failed to do so himself in the moment.
quotes.key_insight: The tech and startup community must actively confront casual sexism rather than nervously laughing it off, because tolerating it drives women out of the industry.

quotes.quote: And if you see someone doing this, call them on it. I didn't… that was my nervous laughter in the background of the recording.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro calls out casual sexism in the startup and tech community, recounting an incident where a well-credentialed female panel moderator was introduced by referencing her appearance and marital status rather than her professional accomplishments. He cites additional examples of objectifying behavior he's witnessed, including a Techstars team using gratuitous photos of women in a pitch deck and a founder commenting on a receptionist's appearance. He argues that tolerating this behavior will drive away women tech entrepreneurs and urges people to speak up when they see it, while admitting he failed to do so himself in the moment.
quotes.key_insight: The tech and startup community must actively confront casual sexism rather than nervously laughing it off, because tolerating it drives women out of the industry.

quotes.quote: As with all good things, it came about via a stupid coincidence.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts crashing a 500 Startups demo day after a chance encounter with a former cofounder on a flight to San Jose. He provides brief reviews of standout companies from the batch of 32 startups, spanning consumer apps, content personalization, fitness gamification, and children's media. The post captures the energy and eclecticism of early-stage startup culture circa 2012, with Shapiro offering candid takes on each company's strengths and weaknesses. He highlights the global reach of 500 Startups, noting companies from Japan to Brazil in the portfolio.
quotes.key_insight: A strong demo day showcases not just ideas but founder stories and creative positioning, with the best pitches combining clear market insight with memorable presentation.

quotes.quote: VCs don't actually like their companies to be profitable. Someday, sure, but not on their watch.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro explains why most businesses—using a taxi company as a concrete example—are fundamentally unsuitable for venture capital funding. Prompted by an email from a Boston taxi operator seeking VC, he lays out six reasons: VCs want explosive growth over profitability, demand outrageous margins, need 10x returns, prefer investing in elite insiders, require months of full-time fundraising with low success rates, and impose board oversight that limits founder autonomy. He then outlines alternatives including angel investors, bank loans, revenue-based financing, and bootstrapping, while acknowledging that VC is genuinely powerful for the rare company that fits the model.
quotes.key_insight: Venture capital is designed for a tiny fraction of businesses that can achieve explosive growth with outrageous margins, and most founders would be better served by alternatives like bootstrapping, angel investment, or revenue-based lending.

quotes.quote: When the day comes that a VC-backed business generates cash faster than it can effectively spend it? They sell the company, or IPO, or replace the CEO with someone who can spend faster.

quotes.quote: I decided that it was a rare occurrence in this world that you actually get to save the life of a stranger, and if skeleton-draining was the price of that, then so be it.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts how he registered as a bone marrow donor in college in 1995, then lost track of his registration as his contact information changed over 16 years. After reading about Amit Gupta's leukemia diagnosis on Hacker News, he tried to update his donor registry info but gave up. Months later, while sorting through old mail from his acquired startup Sparkbuy, he discovered the marrow registry had found a match for him — but the letter had sat unread for five months. He called immediately but was told the patient likely found another donor or was 'no longer eligible,' a grim euphemism. The post serves as a urgent plea to join the marrow registry and keep your contact information current.
quotes.key_insight: The bureaucratic friction of outdated contact information and ordinary procrastination can quietly nullify a life-saving commitment made years earlier.

quotes.quote: A kid's adorable with raspberry juice on his face, but your business should put its best foot forward.
quotes.summary: Shapiro notices that Sprouter's website looks subtly amateurish and traces the problem to JPEG compression artifacts in their logo. He compares logo schmutz to a kid with raspberry juice on his face — endearing on a child, unprofessional on a business. The fix is simple: use PNG format for logos instead of JPEG, since PNG offers lossless compression that keeps edges clean. He notes that while most visitors won't consciously notice compression artifacts, the subtle cues still signal whether a company looks professional or homegrown. The post is a quick, practical reminder that small design details shape perception.
quotes.key_insight: Tiny design oversights like JPEG artifacts on your logo silently signal amateurism, and fixing them is one of the cheapest ways to look like a real company.

quotes.quote: There's no way to screw it up faster than going around asking, 'Hey, could you introduce me to some investors?'
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro argues that asking contacts for generic introductions to investors is counterproductive and marks you as inexperienced. He outlines three reasons: not all investors are right for your company, it's lazy to offload the matchmaking work to your contact, and unprepared introductions will be lukewarm and ineffective. Instead, he prescribes a five-step process: research specific investors beforehand, pitch your contact first to build their enthusiasm, ask for specific named introductions, then ask for additional suggestions, and follow up with forwardable emails. The core message is that fundraising requires the same rigor and preparation as building the product itself.
quotes.key_insight: Fundraising introductions are a sales process — you must do the research, pitch your contact first, and ask for specific named introductions rather than outsourcing the matchmaking to someone else.

quotes.quote: Since this will apply to everyone submitting expenses, including me and any other board members who are traveling, I wanted to have it officially adopted by a board action.
quotes.summary: Shapiro shares a practical strategy for handling VCs who charge excessive travel expenses to portfolio companies. Rather than confronting the board member directly, he created a company-wide travel expense policy and had the board formally approve it. This allowed the issue to be resolved bureaucratically without personal confrontation or bruised egos. He acknowledges the approach was somewhat passive-aggressive but effective, and adds nuance by noting VCs are generally good people who may not even be aware their admins are booking premium travel.
quotes.key_insight: When a board member's behavior needs correcting, creating a formal policy that applies to everyone is more effective and less damaging than direct confrontation.

quotes.quote: So when a first class ticket came in, we priced how much the coach ticket would have cost, and that was the amount of reimbursement sent.

This allowed the issue to be resolved bureaucratically without personal confrontation or bruised egos. He acknowledges the approach was somewhat passive-aggressive but effective, and adds nuance by noting VCs are generally good people who may not even be aware their admins are booking premium travel.
quotes.key_insight: When a board member's behavior needs correcting, creating a formal policy that applies to everyone is more effective and less damaging than direct confrontation.

quotes.quote: Whenever an investor asks you for your business plan, send them the same damn packet you send to everyone else.
quotes.summary: Shapiro discusses the awkward situation of being asked for a business plan by investors, referencing Brad Feld and Jason Mendelson's book 'Venture Deals' which acknowledges the document is largely obsolete but warns founders to be prepared for the request. Drawing from his experience pitching 70+ VCs and 100+ angels across multiple funding rounds at Ontela, Shapiro notes that no VC ever asked for a business plan, but several angels did during the seed round. His solution: send the same materials you send everyone else—an executive summary and slide deck—without apology or acknowledgment. He notes that investors who ask for business plans are probably not the right fit for high-growth tech startups, as none of the angels who requested one ultimately invested.
quotes.key_insight: When investors ask for a business plan, don't panic or apologize—just send your standard pitch materials, and recognize that the request itself is a signal of investor-founder misalignment.

quotes.quote: Pick up a SIM card from the local mobile store, wireless stand, fast food chain, gas station, vending machine, cabbage shop, or hobo.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his frustrating experience trying to get mobile data working on his Android phone while traveling in the UK. What should be a simple process of buying a prepaid SIM card turns into a 33-step odyssey involving carrier unlock codes, incompatible credit cards, unhelpful phone trees, fraud alerts from American Express, and repeated reboots. The post uses an escalating numbered list format to comedic effect, with each step revealing a new absurd obstacle. The conclusion—raiding the minibar—is the only rational response to a system designed to defeat its users.
quotes.key_insight: The gap between how simple a process should be and how broken it actually is reveals systemic failures in designing for real users rather than ideal ones.

quotes.quote: A Hoverboard Startup is one where the problem is screamingly obvious, but the solution is prohibitively hard.
quotes.summary: Shapiro introduces a framework for categorizing startups into two extreme types: 'Twitter Startups' where the solution is obvious but nobody understands why anyone would want it, and 'Hoverboard Startups' where the problem is obvious but the solution seems impossibly hard. He argues that founders who don't recognize which category they fall into will botch their investor pitches. Twitter Startups should lead with traction and customer validation, while Hoverboard Startups need to demonstrate their technical breakthrough immediately. Most startups fall somewhere in between, but recognizing your type determines how you should structure your pitch.
quotes.key_insight: Founders must recognize whether their startup's core risk is demand (Twitter) or feasibility (Hoverboard), then structure their entire pitch around answering that single question.

quotes.quote: Most startups fail – but of those that are successful, most are acquired.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro explains the multi-faceted reasoning behind selling his startup Sparkbuy to Google just six months after incorporation. The deal provided life-changing personal wealth, great returns for angel investors on a time-adjusted basis, and placed the team at their top-choice acquirer. Shapiro was drawn to pursuing Sparkbuy's product vision at Google scale while also welcoming the stability of a steady paycheck after eight years of startup life. He candidly admits that while raising a large round was the best strategic move for the company, he personally didn't want to do it again. The sale freed him to eventually swing for the fences on future ventures without financial anxiety.
quotes.key_insight: Selling a startup can be the right decision when the founder's personal goals diverge from the company's optimal strategic path, especially when the deal simultaneously rewards investors, provides life-changing financial security, and frees the founder to take bigger risks next time.

quotes.quote: If I was going to go to work for The Man, I was more excited about The Man being Google than anyone else.

quotes.quote: I'm a big believer that economic incentives shape behaviors in subtle and unmeasurable ways.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro writes an open letter to TechCrunch founder Mike Arrington, asking him to stop investing in startups because it creates conflicts of interest with his journalism. Shapiro argues that economic incentives shape behavior in subtle, unmeasurable ways, and that Arrington's dual role as investor and reporter will inevitably compromise TechCrunch's editorial independence. He frames journalistic independence as a product feature that shouldn't be sacrificed. The letter is notably polite and brief, positioning itself as customer feedback rather than a moral indictment.
quotes.key_insight: Journalistic independence is best understood not as an abstract ethical ideal but as a core product feature — and conflicts of interest degrade it in ways that are subtle but inevitable.

quotes.quote: I think your decision to make investments in startups is going to make TechCrunch a worse product.

quotes.quote: The question of equity brings out the most fundamental differences, perceptions, and values in an aspiring startup. In fact the equity question, more than any other, may strangle a young company before it can even get started. And that's a damn good thing.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro argues that equal (50/50) cofounder equity splits are always wrong because they represent avoidance of hard conversations rather than a thoughtful business decision. He presents a formula for calculating equity splits based on factors like who initiated the startup, who brought the idea, prior execution, CEO role, full-time commitment, reputation, and cash investment. He contends that unequal splits force cofounders to confront difficult questions about relative contributions early, which is healthy for the company. He directly rebuts Joel Spolsky's advocacy for equal splits, arguing it confuses 'easy' with 'fair' and doesn't reflect market realities. The core message is that cofounders who can't resolve equity disagreements should discover that incompatibility before taking on investors and employees.
quotes.key_insight: A 50/50 equity split is conflict avoidance masquerading as fairness — cofounders must confront uncomfortable differences in contribution and risk early, because the inability to resolve those questions is itself the strongest signal the partnership will fail.

quotes.quote: The rule is this: if you're working for a company that's so young it can't pay you, you're a founder. If you are drawing a salary on your first day at work, you're not.

quotes.quote: A great startup with a dozen or so people will typically pay its employees about a third less than a big company.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro breaks down the real value of startup stock options, arguing that most employees dramatically overestimate what their equity is worth. He explains how liquidation preferences, participation rights, and dilution from funding rounds significantly erode the value of common shares compared to preferred investor shares. The post provides a practical heuristic formula for estimating option value that accounts for company failure risk, exit expectations, capital raised, and investor-friendly terms. Through a worked example, he shows that 0.1% of a company expecting a $250-500mm exit might only be worth $35k after adjustments. His motivation is transparency: he believes informed candidates will recognize genuinely good equity offers.
quotes.key_insight: Startup stock options are worth far less than headline valuations suggest once you account for liquidation preferences, dilution, and the base rate of startup failure, and employees should demand transparency to evaluate equity offers realistically.

quotes.quote: The existence of preferred, 'investor' shares significantly devalues regular, common shares.

quotes.quote: If you're building a secret Twitter account, be sure to use a secret email address for it too, OK?
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro discovered a job candidate's secret Twitter account simply by using tools like Gist and Rapportive that link email addresses to social media profiles. While reviewing an otherwise outstanding student resume, he found the candidate's hidden Twitter account through an email-based API lookup — not through googling. The post serves as a cautionary tale about online privacy: your email address is a skeleton key to your digital identity. Shapiro warns that anyone who thinks their anonymous social media accounts are truly separate from their professional identity is mistaken if they used the same email address.
quotes.key_insight: Your email address is the thread that ties all your online identities together, and readily available tools can pull that thread with a single click.

quotes.quote: Patent drawings are mostly similar to high school notebook doodles except that they cost $5,000.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro provides a practical guide to quickly evaluating a patent by skipping most of its content. He argues that titles, drawings, abstracts, and specifications are largely misleading or incomprehensible, and that the only legally enforceable part worth reading is the independent claims. The post walks through each section of a patent, explaining why it can be safely ignored, with humor and real examples. He notes that even the patent office itself reads patents this way—claims first, then drawings, then specification only if needed.
quotes.key_insight: The only legally enforceable part of a patent is its independent claims, so skip everything else when you need a quick read.

quotes.quote: It's crazy but true: if you know how a VC gets paid, you can pretty much read their mind.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro demystifies seemingly irrational VC behavior by tracing it back to fund economics. He explains that VCs invest other people's money from 10-year funds, earning 2% annual management fees plus 20% of profits, which creates incentive structures that drive counterintuitive decisions. The math of needing 3.6x returns just to match the S&P 500 explains why VCs chase 10x outcomes, push founders to take more capital than needed, block reasonable exits, and herd into the same investment sectors. Shapiro argues that once you understand how VCs get paid, their behavior becomes entirely predictable rather than insane.
quotes.key_insight: VC behavior that appears irrational to entrepreneurs is entirely predictable once you understand fund economics: the 2-20 fee structure, 10-year fund timelines, and the math required to beat the S&P 500.

quotes.quote: If you're a friend, or your followers outnumber your follows, I'll look at your page.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his simple decision tree for deciding who to follow on Twitter. After his account grew unexpectedly when a talk about RC airplanes got picked up by Boing Boing, he developed a quick algorithm to manage incoming follows. The criteria prioritize signal over noise: he checks if someone is a friend or has a good follower-to-following ratio, then evaluates tweet frequency and interestingness.
quotes.key_insight: A simple heuristic based on signal-to-noise ratio — checking follower ratios, tweet frequency, and content quality — is enough to manage a growing Twitter following.

quotes.quote: If you don't tweet too often and at least a third of your tweets are interesting (and private exchanges with someone do not qualify), then I follow you.

After his account grew unexpectedly when a talk about RC airplanes got picked up by Boing Boing, he developed a quick algorithm to manage incoming follows. The criteria prioritize signal over noise: he checks if someone is a friend or has a good follower-to-following ratio, then evaluates tweet frequency and interestingness.
quotes.key_insight: A simple heuristic based on signal-to-noise ratio — checking follower ratios, tweet frequency, and content quality — is enough to manage a growing Twitter following.

quotes.quote: Company Culture is a very serious matter, put together after much employee feedback and deliberation, and carefully designed to capture the key things that make your company great. It's also a load of well-mixed fertilizer.
quotes.summary: Shapiro argues that most company culture statements are meaningless platitudes—generic aspirations like 'hire the best' and 'value teamwork' that every company claims. Real culture, he contends, is defined not by what makes a company great but by what makes it different. He identifies four components of authentic culture: polarizing decisions (refusing to compromise between opposing imperatives), excesses (taking good things to extremes), quirks (weird distinctions integral to the employee experience), and dysfunctions (the mirror-image costs of cultural choices). The framework implies that if your culture statement could belong to any company, it's not actually describing your culture.
quotes.key_insight: Authentic company culture is defined by the polarizing trade-offs a company refuses to compromise on, not by the universally admirable values every company claims.

quotes.quote: The Rule of Company Culture: It's what makes your company different, not what makes it great.

quotes.quote: If you find yourself saying 'we can do it all', that's great! And you're right, sort of. Your attempts at balance are admirable and may be successful, but do not constitute a corporate culture.
quotes.summary: Shapiro argues that most company culture statements are meaningless platitudes—generic aspirations like 'hire the best' and 'value teamwork' that every company claims. Real culture, he contends, is defined not by what makes a company great but by what makes it different. He identifies four components of authentic culture: polarizing decisions (refusing to compromise between opposing imperatives), excesses (taking good things to extremes), quirks (weird distinctions integral to the employee experience), and dysfunctions (the mirror-image costs of cultural choices). The framework implies that if your culture statement could belong to any company, it's not actually describing your culture.
quotes.key_insight: Authentic company culture is defined by the polarizing trade-offs a company refuses to compromise on, not by the universally admirable values every company claims.

quotes.quote: That only comes from taking a stand on one end of the see-saw.

quotes.quote: What you have to realize in business is that in some things you're the Chairman, and in some you're the boor.
quotes.summary: Shapiro contrasts two VCs telling nationality jokes at conferences — one (Tom Huseby) who brought the room together with laughter and built relationships, and another who offended people and killed the mood. He uses this to argue that most business advice is bad not because it's wrong, but because it's context-dependent: what works brilliantly for one leader's personality and relationship style can backfire catastrophically for another. The core lesson is that leaders must honestly assess which behaviors suit their own style rather than blindly copying what works for someone else.
quotes.key_insight: Business advice is only as good as its fit with your own leadership style — the critical skill is honestly knowing which tactics suit your persona and which will make you look foolish.

quotes.quote: B'H? This is not something you see in your typical Nigerian con.
quotes.summary: Shapiro describes a family member's Gmail account being hacked and used to send a scam email requesting money. Unlike typical spam, this attack was more sophisticated: the hacker forwarded future emails to themselves, deleted contacts, and most tellingly, included 'B'H' — a Jewish religious abbreviation — in the scam message. Shapiro reasons that since Jews represent only 0.2% of the world population, including this marker suggests the hackers specifically targeted Jewish victims. He speculates the attackers may have screened for Jewish last names when selecting accounts to compromise.
quotes.key_insight: A Jewish religious marker in a phishing scam reveals that hackers may be ethnically profiling their targets to craft more convincing social engineering attacks.

quotes.quote: 2% of the US population is Jewish. 0.2% of the world population is Jewish. This is a seriously weird thing to put in an email designed to part people with their money.

quotes.quote: Those companies need to attract armies. You only need to hire one.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro argues that startups should embrace polarization in recruiting rather than trying to appeal to everyone. Using the example of founding Ontela, he explains how they deliberately chose to locate on Seattle's Westside despite most tech talent being on the Eastside near Microsoft. This decision alienated over half their potential candidates but made the remaining minority enthusiastic about the opportunity. The strategy proved instrumental in making early hires, including a senior developer who joined largely because the shorter commute meant more time with his kids. The core lesson is that startups should leverage their uniqueness as a feature, not a bug.
quotes.key_insight: Startups win talent not by competing head-to-head with big companies for the broad market, but by making polarizing choices that deeply resonate with a passionate few.

quotes.quote: It's vastly better to polarize the market, alienate half your potential work force, and buck conventional wisdom than it is to play to the middle.

quotes.quote: What with being gainfully unemployed and all, I've decided to revisit my blog.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he's reviving his blog after leaving Photobucket to pursue a new business idea. The blog was previously dedicated to woodworking under the name 'Nothing Severed Yet,' but he signals a shift toward more varied content. The old woodworking posts remain archived. It's a brief, transitional post marking the start of a new chapter.
quotes.key_insight: A lighthearted reintroduction post marking Shapiro's transition from corporate life to independent entrepreneurship, pivoting his blog from woodworking to broader topics.

quotes.quote: Those of you with long memories or penchants for manly crafts may remember this URL as the home of 'Nothing Severed Yet', my woodworking blog.
