quotes.quote: Scrape with a cabinet scraper – wiping it fairly often. It doesn't chip like using a chisel, and it doesn't take nearly as long or make nearly as much mess as wiping.
quotes.summary: This brief post shares a reader's tip for removing glue from woodworking glueups. Jacques Richer suggests using a cabinet scraper as a superior alternative to chiseling or wiping. The technique involves waiting about 90 minutes for the glue to reach a semi-solid state, then scraping it off cleanly. It's presented as a practical update to an earlier article on the same topic.
quotes.key_insight: A cabinet scraper applied to semi-cured glue offers the cleanest and most efficient method for removing squeeze-out from woodworking glueups.

quotes.quote: Unfortunately, I then decided to get clever.
quotes.summary: Shapiro follows up on his coffee table project that embeds slate tiles into wood. He describes the finishing process using Minwax Polycrylic, noting the care needed to avoid bubbles on rough slate surfaces. He recounts a mistake of waxing the tiles, which caused buildup in crevices that made them look dirty. After removing the wax manually, he concludes the tiles work great as drink coasters and recommends the technique for future projects.
quotes.key_insight: Embedded slate tiles in wood furniture work beautifully as built-in coasters, but resist the urge to over-finish them — getting clever with extra coatings can create more problems than it solves.

quotes.quote: The wax built up in the crevices and was very noticable. It made the things look dirty.

quotes.quote: If veneer is cool, well, why not make the speakers out of solid wood?
quotes.summary: Shapiro reports from CES 2006 on a trend in speaker manufacturing: overseas companies discovered that applying beautiful wood veneer over acoustically-dead MDF produces gorgeous, great-sounding, affordable floorstanding speakers. However, some manufacturers took this too far by building speakers from solid wood, which is both more expensive and acoustically inferior to MDF since wood fibers resonate and distort sound. The post serves as both a CES trend report and a practical lesson for woodworkers.
quotes.key_insight: The most expensive material isn't always the best — veneer over MDF beats solid wood for speakers on cost, aesthetics, and acoustics, but manufacturers chase perceived premium over actual performance.

quotes.quote: Solid wood is actually worse for speakers than MDF, because the wood fibers resonate and mess up the acoustics.

quotes.quote: Several months ago I founded a company with a friend.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces his return to blogging after a months-long absence caused by secretly founding a new company, Ontela Inc., while maintaining his day job. He explains he couldn't discuss the venture publicly while still employed elsewhere. As of the post date, he's transitioning to work at Ontela full-time, which he hopes will free up small amounts of time for his woodworking hobby and blog.
quotes.key_insight: An early glimpse of Dan Shapiro's entrepreneurial trajectory, as he leaves his day job to go full-time on his startup Ontela—a move that would become a recurring pattern in his career.

quotes.quote: I will be working my butt off at just one job instead of two, leaving dozens of minutes a week for woodworking and blogging and personal hygene.

quotes.quote: There is no way to remove *all* of the paint without also removing a serious portion of the wood.
quotes.summary: Shapiro recounts spending two weeks stripping paint from exposed beams in his living room and arriving at a hard-won conclusion: it's not worth it. Despite trying every method—liquid strippers, gel strippers, sanding, scraping, steel wool—he found that removing all five layers of paint without damaging the wood is essentially impossible. He humorously proposes a three-step alternative: throw out the painted wood, buy unfinished wood, and apply finish.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the most practical solution to a restoration problem is to stop restoring and start replacing.

quotes.quote: So the only tool that indiscriminately removes paint is the belt sander.

quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the most practical solution to a restoration problem is to stop restoring and start replacing.

quotes.quote: If you want any other look then follow my simple 3 step process.

quotes.quote: My non-woodworking real life has been too busy to spend much time in the shop.
quotes.summary: A brief housekeeping post where Dan Shapiro apologizes for not posting on his woodworking blog. He explains that his day job has been too busy and he just returned from a vacation in Vietnam. He promises to resume posting but warns updates may remain sporadic.
quotes.key_insight: A brief housekeeping note from Shapiro's early woodworking blog era, notable only as a snapshot of his pre-tech-CEO blogging life.

quotes.quote: Heat is the enemy of any battery pack.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews Bosch's new BLUECORE battery system for cordless tools, which offers 50% longer battery life and 30-minute charging through embedded heat rods and a microchip that communicates with the charger. He highlights the backward compatibility as the standout feature, noting that existing Bosch cordless tool owners can upgrade their batteries and chargers without replacing their entire tool collection. The post praises Bosch for not abandoning existing customers when introducing new technology.
quotes.key_insight: The best technology upgrades are ones that enhance what customers already own rather than forcing them to start over.

quotes.quote: Instead of squeezing you to buy a whole new set of tools to get these advantages you can just replace your existing packs as they wear out.

quotes.quote: It seems that sticking a router under a hole in a table and using it from the top should be a simple bit of engineering, but until recently it eluded most router manufactures.
quotes.summary: This is an early blog post from Dan Shapiro's site featuring a guest review by Brian Todoroff of the Bosch RA1165 undertable router base. The review praises the product as an affordable alternative to expensive router lifts, allowing above-table height adjustment for under $60. The reviewer notes minor issues with locating the drill hole for the adjustment screw and occasional router tilting when the latch is released. Overall, the product is recommended as a great value for hobbyist woodworkers who don't need a full professional router lift.
quotes.key_insight: A guest woodworking product review from Dan Shapiro's early blogging days, showcasing his longstanding connection to maker culture and hands-on engineering.

quotes.quote: That was before the ingenious discovery by the book industry that people prefered to buy introductory reading material from authors who considered the buyer to be 'a person of profound mental retardation having a mental age below three years.'
quotes.summary: Shapiro humorously critiques the publishing industry's trend of titling introductory books with insulting names (like 'for Dummies' or 'for Idiots'), contrasting them with older, straightforwardly titled reference books. He then reviews 'Basic Woodworking' by Sunset Books, a concise, unpretentious reference guide that covers essential woodworking topics in 1-5 pages each. He praises the book's efficiency and utility as a first-check reference for practical woodworking questions. The post is a lighthearted book recommendation wrapped in sharp commentary on marketing trends.
quotes.key_insight: The best reference books are concise and respect their readers' intelligence rather than padding content or condescending through clever branding.

quotes.quote: The people who invented it thought of every tomfool way you could bollox up your woodworking projects with their product, and engineered around it.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews the DowelMax doweling jig, a $230 woodworking tool he considers worth every penny despite its high price. He argues it eliminates the most painful phase of woodworking—alignment and glueup—by making it virtually impossible to drill inaccurate dowel holes. He demonstrates its precision by drilling 90 holes across six boards with perfect alignment, and its strength by showing dowel joints outlasting biscuit joints in destructive testing. The review is structured as a product endorsement grounded in hands-on shop experience.
quotes.key_insight: A precision tool that eliminates the most error-prone phase of woodworking—alignment and glueup—is worth a premium price because it transforms an anxiety-ridden process into a reliable one.

quotes.quote: Sometimes something comes along that's just so odd, I have to share it with everyone, even though there isn't even the faintest connection between woodworking and the subject at hand.

He acknowledges it's completely off-topic from his blog's usual woodworking focus but feels compelled to share it because it's so entertaining. The post is essentially a link-share with minimal commentary, reflecting the casual, personal blogging style common in 2005.
quotes.key_insight: A lighthearted off-topic post sharing a humorous airline complaint letter, notable mainly as an artifact of early blogging culture.

quotes.quote: if I buy four bf of snakewood, I've just saved enough to buy a new drill press. Brilliant!
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recommends Woodweb's forums as an online marketplace for finding lumber deals, from reclaimed antique timbers to exotic woods like snakewood at steep discounts. He notes that savings on rare wood can effectively pay for new tools. He advises caution due to occasional scams, suggesting buyers ask for references and buy locally when possible to avoid shipping costs and verify quality in person.
quotes.key_insight: Online lumber forums can offer exotic woods at a fraction of retail price, but buyers should verify sellers and inspect goods before purchasing.

quotes.quote: It's important to be careful since there are a few ripoff stories floating around.

quotes.quote: It turns out that if you have a hole that goes all the way through the board, epoxy (even the thick stuff) will drain right through to the other side.
quotes.summary: Shapiro shares a practical woodworking tip about filling holes in walnut burl using clear epoxy. He walks through the iterative problem-solving process: epoxy drains through holes that penetrate the board, taping the back traps air bubbles, and the solution is a pinhole in the tape that gets sealed once epoxy starts dripping. It's a short, hands-on maker post from early in his blogging history.
quotes.key_insight: Iterative problem-solving in hands-on making often means discovering that each fix introduces a new constraint, and the best solutions address multiple problems elegantly.

quotes.quote: It turns out that if you do that, there's nowhere for the air trapped in the hole to go, and you wind up with a big bubble at the bottom of the hole.

quotes.quote: I've been making dovetails for the past week, which means I've screwed up a lot of dovetails.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical lessons learned from a week of cutting dovetail joints, a traditional woodworking technique. The post catalogs ten specific tips covering material preparation, tool calibration, gluing technique, clamping strategy, and salvaging mistakes. Written in a casual, numbered-list format, it reflects hands-on trial and error at the workbench. The overall message is that dovetails require precision at every step, but mistakes are recoverable with the right tricks.
quotes.key_insight: Dovetail joinery demands precision in preparation and patience in execution, but nearly every mistake can be salvaged with clever gluing, clamping, and filling techniques.

quotes.quote: There's two things to understand about the Incra system. One is that it is a router fence, not a jig.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro explains the Incra positioning system for woodworking, demystifying how it differs from traditional dovetail jigs. He breaks down its three operating modes: micro-positioning for thousandth-of-an-inch adjustments, free sliding for large movements, and the key 'lock in' mode that snaps to the nearest 1/32 inch. The post argues that this snap-to-position mechanism enables perfect repeatability for dovetail joints. He concludes with a strong endorsement, noting that every woodworker he knows has adopted the system.
quotes.key_insight: The Incra positioning system's genius lies in its snap-to-position lock-in mode, which transforms imprecise manual movements into perfectly repeatable precision cuts.

quotes.quote: You can make a cut, slide it just about 1″ away, and it will snap to exactly 1″ away. Then you can move it back to your first cut, and it will snap to exactly where your first cut was. Over and over again, perfect repeatability.

quotes.quote: Evidently I'm not the only woodworker who has contemplated building a poker table… actually, judging by this site, I may be the only one who hasn't done it yet.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a resource he discovered that compiles dozens of DIY poker table building projects from woodworkers across the web. He notes the variety of designs and sizes available, covering different card games, and highlights that most include instructions and all have pictures. The post is a brief, enthusiastic recommendation for fellow woodworkers interested in building their own poker tables, with Shapiro humorously admitting he may be the last woodworker who hasn't built one yet.
quotes.key_insight: The maker community freely shares detailed build plans, making ambitious woodworking projects accessible to anyone willing to try.

quotes.quote: Being an arrogant engineer by day, I pride myself on understanding how things really work, but these beauties make no sense whatsoever.
quotes.summary: A guest post by Brian Todoroff reviewing PowerTwist Plus V-Belts for woodworking machinery. The post marvels at how these segmented, link-based belts produce smoother and quieter operation than traditional smooth belts, defying intuitive engineering logic. The belts are modular, allowing users to create any length by adding or removing segments, and can be swapped between machines. Todoroff recommends them as an easy, cost-effective upgrade for any belt-driven workshop machinery.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the best engineering solutions are counterintuitive — a segmented, rough-looking belt can outperform a smooth one for vibration reduction in workshop machinery.

quotes.quote: You'd think that for a smooth running belt you'd want, well… a smooth belt.

quotes.quote: In a twist of logic worthy of Douglas Adams, these belts are about as far from smooth as possible.
quotes.summary: A guest post by Brian Todoroff reviewing PowerTwist Plus V-Belts for woodworking machinery. The post marvels at how these segmented, link-based belts produce smoother and quieter operation than traditional smooth belts, defying intuitive engineering logic. The belts are modular, allowing users to create any length by adding or removing segments, and can be swapped between machines. Todoroff recommends them as an easy, cost-effective upgrade for any belt-driven workshop machinery.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the best engineering solutions are counterintuitive — a segmented, rough-looking belt can outperform a smooth one for vibration reduction in workshop machinery.

quotes.quote: My old Grizzly contractor saw was so loud that I had neighbors complain when I fired it up. Admittedly it was one in the morning. But still.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical advice for reducing tablesaw noise in a home workshop. He describes his own journey from a loud Grizzly contractor saw that annoyed neighbors to a quiet cabinet saw that lets him work while his wife sleeps. He offers three tiers of solutions: upgrading to a cabinet saw with its enclosed base and heavier construction, aligning your existing saw to reduce vibration, and adding accessories like link belts, zero-clearance inserts, and premium blades. The post is a concise, opinionated guide from an enthusiastic woodworker.
quotes.key_insight: A quieter tablesaw comes from addressing noise at every level — the machine itself, its alignment, and its accessories — turning a shop disruption into a non-issue.

quotes.quote: I hate brushing finishes. I don't dislike it, or prefer not to do it. I flat out despise it.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro delivers a passionate rant against brushing finishes in woodworking, detailing every frustrating step from mixing to cleaning to recoating. He walks through the problems with getting the right consistency, maintaining expensive brushes, dealing with bubbles, and the technique required to avoid drips and runs. His alternative recommendation is to use wipe-on or spray-on finishes with cheaper, disposable applicators. The post is a practical maker's guide disguised as a comedic complaint.
quotes.key_insight: The frustrations of brush-on finishes in woodworking can be entirely sidestepped by switching to wipe-on or spray-on alternatives with disposable applicators.

quotes.quote: Brushing poses all sorts of problems, or shall I say, 'technique-enhancing opportunities'.

quotes.quote: Don't let all the FUD fool you; brushing is a great way to apply many types of finish, and it's not a lot more work than most other techniques.
quotes.summary: This early blog post is a practical guide to choosing quality finishing brushes for woodworking projects. Todoroff (a contributing editor posted on Shapiro's blog) explains the three key features of a good brush: a fluid reservoir, long bent bristles, and flagged ends. The post argues that investing in a quality brush is essential because it's the last tool to touch a finished project. It also covers the practical distinction between natural and synthetic bristles, recommending natural for oil-based finishes and synthetic for water-based ones.
quotes.key_insight: The quality of your finishing brush—defined by its fluid reservoir, bent bristles, and flagged ends—is the most important factor in achieving a good brushed finish on woodworking projects.

quotes.quote: Where most people get into trouble when brushing is either rushing successive coats or using a cheap brush.

quotes.quote: I'm also trying something a little different. I've included the source materials that I used to make this.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares detailed plans for building a portable humidor made from walnut and cocobolo wood. The post provides an 11-page PDF with diagrams, photos, and step-by-step instructions. He also experiments with open-source sharing by releasing all source materials—including the publisher file, full-resolution photos, and 3D model files—under a Creative Commons license. He encourages others to remix, translate, or repurpose the materials however they see fit.
quotes.key_insight: Sharing not just finished creative work but all source materials under open licenses invites community participation and remix—an early expression of the maker culture ethos.

quotes.quote: Taunton has always managed to bring a deep sense of quality to everything they produce.
quotes.summary: This is a guest book review by contributing editor Brian Todoroff reviewing Taunton Press's 'The New Best of Fine Woodworking' book series. The series collects selected articles from Fine Woodworking Magazine over the past decade, organized by topic. The reviewer finds that the topical grouping adds value even for existing magazine subscribers. Despite minor editing errors, the books are recommended for both new and experienced woodworkers seeking consolidated reference material on specific techniques.
quotes.key_insight: Curating and organizing existing content by topic creates new value beyond the sum of its parts, making reference material more accessible and actionable for practitioners at all levels.

quotes.quote: Generally they are a pain. The goal is to find something that passes for being interesting on the show floor, while spending as little time as possible listening to inane sales pitches for things you don't care about and wouldn't take for free let alone pay money for.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his visit to The Woodworking Shows, contrasting it favorably with the tech trade shows he attends for work. Unlike typical industry conventions filled with inane pitches and badge-swiping salespeople, the woodworking show features inventors personally demonstrating their tools and inviting hands-on participation. The show also offers substantive hour-long workshops that go beyond product promotion to teach genuine techniques. Shapiro's highlight was meeting the inventor of his Incra router jig and learning a trick to cut finger joints four times faster.
quotes.key_insight: The best trade shows succeed by replacing abstract sales pitches with hands-on demonstrations and genuine knowledge sharing between makers.

quotes.quote: It's a game where the losers are deluged by junkmail forever.

Shapiro vouches for PSI based on past positive ordering experiences. The post is written in a playful tone under the byline of 'Nothing Severed Yet,' a woodworking publication.
quotes.key_insight: A lighthearted woodworking tip reflecting Shapiro's maker roots long before his tech career took center stage.

quotes.quote: Ebony is notoriously temperamental when it comes to working it and has a nasty reputation for suddenly splitting long after you believe all danger is past.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro describes his search for a black wood alternative for a contemporary wall case design. After considering temperamental ebony and chemically treated walnut, he discovers that India ink provides an excellent solution for blackening wood. He experiments with it on birch plywood and finds it produces an impressively deep black finish with minimal effort, preserving grain texture and holding up well under top coating. The post concludes with his satisfaction at finding a practical, safe, and effective ebony substitute.
quotes.key_insight: India ink offers woodworkers a safe, permanent, and remarkably effective alternative to ebony, producing a deep black finish that preserves wood grain and accepts top coats without bleeding.

quotes.quote: Another bonus is that carbon, being an element, simply won't fade and is as black as black gets.

quotes.quote: For now though, I do believe I've found my ebony substitute.

He experiments with it on birch plywood and finds it produces an impressively deep black finish with minimal effort, preserving grain texture and holding up well under top coating. The post concludes with his satisfaction at finding a practical, safe, and effective ebony substitute.

quotes.quote: It's great to poke around the stacks and discover that one spectacular piece with the burl inclusion and a bit of crossgrain ripple that just screams 'jewelry box!' in a voice that only you can hear.
quotes.summary: This guest post by Brian Todoroff reviews the experience of ordering lumber online rather than buying from local suppliers. Living in the San Francisco Bay Area where wood prices are high and selection is poor, Brian discovered that mail-order wood could beat local prices even after shipping while offering broader selection. He reviews two suppliers — New England Specialty Lumber Supply (a small operation with great prices and personal service) and Woodworker's Source (a larger operation with excellent selection and quality). The post covers practical considerations like rough vs. surfaced lumber, shipping weight thresholds, and quality expectations.
quotes.key_insight: Mail-order lumber is a viable and often superior alternative to local suppliers, offering better prices and selection despite the counterintuitive economics of shipping heavy wood cross-country.

quotes.quote: It always seemed like a silly and expensive thing to try to ship a good chunk of a tree cross-country, sight unseen, in hopes of getting something workable.

quotes.quote: In some dark corner that you tell no one about is also that colossal waste of money. Perhaps it has long since been forcefully ejected from your sawdust kingdom, but the memory of the wonderful project it ruined still haunts you.
quotes.summary: This early blog post reflects on the invisible craftsmanship behind well-designed tools. Shapiro (via contributing editor Brian Todoroff) argues that good designers agonize over countless small details that most users never consciously notice. The post uses a woodworking depth stop as a case study, featuring designer Brian Giffin's explanation of why he made specific material and geometry choices. The conclusion is an appreciation for the unseen engineering effort that makes everyday tools work seamlessly.
quotes.key_insight: The best product design is invisible — great tools work so well because their designers obsessed over details that users will never consciously notice.

quotes.quote: How did they know to add that little curve to give your thumb just enough clearance? How did they come up with the million other details that make everything come together so smoothly?

quotes.quote: In terms of usefullness-per-dollar, this sucker is near the top of my list.
quotes.summary: Shapiro compares the Dowelmax doweling jig against a biscuit joiner in a head-to-head woodworking joint strength test. He glued up two joints in a walnut board—one with dowels, one with biscuits—and after a week of curing, smashed the board against a pipe. The biscuit joint broke on the first impact while the dowel joint held. He also notes the Dowelmax produced a perfectly flush joint with minimal effort, while the biscuit joint was off by 1/16th of an inch. His verdict is unequivocal: the Dowelmax is worth its higher price tag and outperforms cheaper alternatives in accuracy, ease of use, speed, and strength.
quotes.key_insight: A rigorous head-to-head test shows the Dowelmax doweling jig decisively beats biscuit joiners in both alignment accuracy and joint strength, justifying its higher price.

quotes.quote: It absolutely blows away the competition for accuracy, ease of use, speed, and strength.

quotes.key_insight: A brief deal alert highlighting a quality woodworking workbench at half price, reflecting Shapiro's early maker culture interests.

quotes.quote: One of the things most beginning woodworkers don't realize is that wood moves through out the year.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical woodworking tip about wood movement — the fact that wood expands and contracts with humidity changes throughout the year. He explains that wood moves more across the grain than with it, which affects joinery and finishing decisions. He recommends an online tool called the 'Shrinkulator' for calculating dimensional changes in wood based on humidity and wood type.
quotes.key_insight: Wood is a living material that moves seasonally with humidity, and understanding this movement is essential for building furniture that lasts.

quotes.quote: Table tops and other wide pieces can easily change size by 1/8th inch and back through out the year.

quotes.quote: I firmly maintain that these facts are unrelated, because admitting otherwise would mean having to clean my shop.
quotes.summary: This 2005 blog post is a guest review by professional cabinetmaker Mike Ewanchuk of Finger Gloves, a product designed for hand-staining wood. The review compares Finger Gloves against standard disposable latex gloves during a real staining project. Mike finds that Finger Gloves dramatically outperform latex in durability, grip, and reusability, requiring zero changes during a full coat versus six pairs of latex gloves. The post is part of Dan Shapiro's 'free tool review' series on his woodworking blog Nothing Severed Yet.
quotes.key_insight: For hand-staining work, reusable finger-specific gloves can be more durable, practical, and cost-effective than disposable latex gloves.

quotes.quote: Why do woodworking plans always go out of their way to make things difficult?
quotes.summary: Shapiro reviews free poker chip box plans from Rockler, using them as a springboard to rant about woodworking plan design philosophy. He argues that most published woodworking plans are needlessly complex, optimized for commercial manufacturing constraints (cheap lumber, expensive labor) rather than hobbyist workshops where the opposite is true. He advocates for simple designs using quality solid wood over complex joinery holding together plywood. The Rockler box plan earns his praise for using a clever shortcut (pre-made walnut molding) to achieve an impressive look without unnecessary complexity.
quotes.key_insight: Good woodworking design for hobbyists means investing in quality materials and simple construction rather than mimicking commercial manufacturing's complex joinery on cheap substrates.

quotes.quote: I felt like someone took pride in the quality of the tool, not just how cheaply they could make it.
quotes.summary: This is a guest review by Brian Todoroff on the Nothing Severed Yet blog, evaluating the Jack Rabbit Deluxe Set, a tool designed to eliminate the tedious process of switching between drill bits and driver bits when drilling, countersinking, and driving screws. The review was prompted by a pantry-building project that required 40 screws and caused hand fatigue from constant bit switching. The reviewer praises the tool's machining quality, thoughtful design, and ease of use, noting it reduces the drill-to-drive switch to under a second. The only criticism is a depth stop shape that can leave slight crescent marks on wood surfaces. The tool earns a 4 out of 5 rating.
quotes.key_insight: A well-designed specialty tool that solves a specific workflow friction point can eliminate the perceived need for additional equipment entirely.

quotes.quote: Or you could just skip the whole shellac thing and follow the instructions on the can, instead of being difficult like me.
quotes.summary: Shapiro provides a technical correction to his earlier woodworking advice about finishing with Behlen's Rockhard Table Top Varnish. He discovered that his recommended finishing schedule of mineral oil, shellac, then varnish doesn't work because the varnish fails to adhere properly to the shellac. He offers three corrective steps: skip mineral oil or use a hardening oil, scuff the shellac before varnishing, and don't thin the first coat over shellac. He self-deprecatingly admits he should have just followed the instructions on the can.
quotes.key_insight: When experimenting with wood finishing techniques, sometimes the best approach is to follow the manufacturer's instructions rather than improvising a complex multi-step schedule.

quotes.key_insight: When experimenting with wood finishing techniques, sometimes the best approach is to follow the manufacturer's instructions rather than improvising a complex multi-step schedule.

quotes.quote: As you may have gathered, I am not a fabulous finisher. But the Rockhard is a pretty fabulous finish.
quotes.summary: Shapiro provides a technical correction to his earlier woodworking advice about finishing with Behlen's Rockhard Table Top Varnish. He discovered that his recommended finishing schedule of mineral oil, shellac, then varnish doesn't work because the varnish fails to adhere properly to the shellac. He offers three corrective steps: skip mineral oil or use a hardening oil, scuff the shellac before varnishing, and don't thin the first coat over shellac. He self-deprecatingly admits he should have just followed the instructions on the can.
quotes.key_insight: When experimenting with wood finishing techniques, sometimes the best approach is to follow the manufacturer's instructions rather than improvising a complex multi-step schedule.

quotes.quote: I could buy Tide for $14, but the Albertsons house brand is pretty tempting at $7. Sometimes I've found a good deal, sometimes you get what you pay for.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro's blog hosts a guest review by contributor Kevin of the Ridgid 4-gallon mini shop vac, purchased for $70 at Home Depot. The review evaluates the vac on four criteria: price, size, noise level, and fine dust filtration, finding it satisfactory on all counts. The main complaint is the non-standard 1 7/8" hose diameter that is incompatible with other shop vac accessories. Overall, the reviewer considers it a good value for a compact, portable shop vac that performs well for woodworking tasks like sanding built-in bookshelves.
quotes.key_insight: The Ridgid mini shop vac is a solid budget choice for woodworkers who value portability and fine dust filtration, despite a baffling non-standard hose size.

quotes.quote: You can use this to get the latest NSY articles sent directly to your inbox, bypassing that pesky "web" thing completely.

The post is a lighthearted, self-deprecating announcement typical of mid-2000s blogging culture.

quotes.quote: In fact, it was the article that had the greatest impact on me when I wrote this piece about chisel shopping.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro highlights a chisel review by David Wright that he considers the best he's ever read. The post serves as a brief recommendation, linking to Wright's PDF comparison of high-end chisels. Shapiro notes that Wright's review significantly influenced his own earlier writing about chisel shopping. Wright adds a postscript confirming his original opinions held up over extended use, with Two Cherries and Stanley 750 remaining his favorites.
quotes.key_insight: A thoughtful initial tool review, when done rigorously, tends to hold up over years of real-world use.

quotes.quote: Stains are basically dilute paint. Dyes actually soak into the fibers, so they don't mask the grain as much as stains do.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his research and practical tips on dyeing a walnut dining room table, explaining the differences between stains and dyes. He covers water-soluble vs. alcohol-soluble dyes, NGR dyes, and techniques for controlling color concentration. The post concludes with a self-deprecating lesson learned about applying water-based dye before mineral oil.
quotes.key_insight: Wood dyes offer superior grain visibility and color control compared to stains, but proper application order and solvent choice are critical to getting good results.

quotes.quote: You can dilute the heck out of dye, and then apply coats of it until it reaches the right color concentration.

quotes.quote: A few weeks ago, my wife told me about this outstanding new service. It's like Netflix, except: They let you have 10 items at a time instead of 3
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro humorously rediscovers the public library through his wife's recommendation, framing it as a Netflix-like service. He then reviews 'Understanding Wood Finishing' by Bob Flexner, praising it as a practical and comprehensive guide to wood finishing techniques. The book covers topics like sanding grits, finish identification, and step-by-step instructions for different woods. His only criticism is the lack of brand-specific recommendations, which he attributes to the book's original 1993 publication date.
quotes.key_insight: A lighthearted book recommendation post that showcases Shapiro's comedic voice and his hands-on maker interests in woodworking.

quotes.quote: I could feel braincells committing harakiri as soon as I opened the thing.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro documents his experiments with Behlen's Rockhard Table Top Varnish as he prepares to finish a walnut dining room table. He tests three different finishing schedules on walnut cutoffs, comparing varnish alone, shellac-then-varnish, and mineral oil-then-shellac-then-varnish combinations. He finds that pre-treating with mineral oil before shellac and varnish produces the deepest, richest color because the low-viscosity oil penetrates the wood grain fully. The post is a hands-on maker's log, complete with observations about the varnish's thick consistency, strong yellow tint, and potent fumes.
quotes.key_insight: The best finish for walnut combines mineral oil for deep penetration and color, shellac to seal it in, and hard varnish on top for durability — each layer serving a distinct purpose.

quotes.quote: Recently my wife has been threatening to stop doing the laundry if she continued to find sawdust filling her laundry baskets.
quotes.summary: A guest post by Brian on Dan Shapiro's blog about a practical workshop improvement. After his wife threatened to stop doing laundry due to sawdust in the baskets, Brian built a wall to separate his garage workshop from the laundry area. He shares his first organizational find: magnetic toolbars from Lee Valley for storing chisels safely and accessibly. Dan adds a budget alternative suggestion from Ikea.
quotes.key_insight: Small, inexpensive organizational tools like magnetic bars can resolve the tension between keeping workshop tools accessible and keeping them safe.

quotes.quote: Since he's built an electric rabbit (VW, not bunny), I'm inclined to believe him.
quotes.summary: This short 2005 post is a reader-contributed correction about lithium-ion battery technology for power tools. Kevin, who built an electric VW Rabbit, corrects Dan's earlier claims about battery chemistry in Milwaukee tools. The discussion covers Li-Ion discharge rates, efficiency, and cost barriers. The post highlights that while Li-Ion batteries offer impressive power-to-weight ratios, their high cost ($145 for a single battery) remained the key obstacle to adoption in consumer power tools at the time.
quotes.key_insight: In 2005, lithium-ion battery technology already had the power and weight advantages to revolutionize cordless tools, but cost was the remaining barrier to widespread adoption.

quotes.quote: For most woodworkers that means that they use fewer feather boards then they really should, or they cheat and end up with hands and fingers too close to hungry blades.
quotes.summary: A guest author reviews the Grip-Tite magnetic feather board, a woodworking safety device invented by hobbyist Jerry Jaksha. The device uses a strong magnet to quickly attach to cast iron tool surfaces, holding wood securely against blades without complex clamping setups. The reviewer argues that its ease of use removes the temptation to skip safety precautions, especially on table saws and jointers. Despite initial sticker shock at $40, hands-on experience at a woodworking class converted the author into an enthusiast who now uses them for nearly every operation.

quotes.quote: When you get lots of lumber, you'll sometimes find small areas with really cool figure.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical woodworking technique for bookmatching lumber using a table saw instead of a bandsaw. The post provides a step-by-step guide for splitting figured wood in half and reassembling it to create symmetrical, decorative patterns. He details the blade height and fence calculations, safety precautions against kickback, and finishing steps to reveal the wood's figure. It's a concise maker tutorial from early in Shapiro's blogging history, well before his AI and tech writing.
quotes.key_insight: You can achieve a decent bookmatch without a bandsaw by making opposing table saw cuts that stop just short of center, then finishing the split by hand.

quotes.quote: During step 4, be sure that the blade isn't cutting all the way through. If it is, the risk of kickback is very high.

quotes.quote: li-ion batteries have more power, but ni-cads have always been able to deliver more power *at once*.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro discusses Milwaukee Tools' announcement of a new 28-volt lithium-ion battery powered cordless tool line. He explains the technical reason the industry hadn't adopted li-ion batteries sooner: while they're lighter and pack more capacity, nickel-cadmium batteries deliver more power instantaneously, which matters for high-draw tools like saws. He expresses cautious optimism about the new tools while lamenting that Milwaukee has been acquired by TTI, a Hong Kong company that also owns Ryobi, marking another American toolmaker moving overseas.
quotes.key_insight: The shift from ni-cad to lithium-ion in power tools wasn't just a matter of adopting better battery chemistry—it required solving the fundamental engineering challenge of instantaneous power delivery for high-current applications.

quotes.quote: You can slap this on in a night.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a simple, affordable wood finishing technique using dewaxed shellac, spray lacquer, and rottenstone polishing. The method produces a tough, scratch-resistant, clear finish that works well even on difficult woods like cocobolo. The entire process can be completed in a single evening with materials costing under $20 from a hardware store. He demonstrates the technique on a portable humidor he built.
quotes.key_insight: A professional-quality wood finish is achievable in one evening with under $20 in materials using a simple shellac-lacquer-rottenstone technique.

quotes.quote: It's tough, scratch-resistant, alcohol and water resistant, and it goes on great over trouble woods like cocobolo.

quotes.quote: It's horsepower and blade quality.
quotes.summary: This is a guest post by Kevin, a woodworker and engineer, reviewing three favorite workshop tools. He advocates for the Porter Cable variable tooth saw blade for its remarkably smooth cuts on a modest table saw, a Husky portable air compressor for its quiet operation and surprising capability despite small size, and the Dremel High Speed Rotary Saw Kit as the definitively superior Dremel model. The post is a practical, no-nonsense set of recommendations from someone who values getting maximum performance from affordable, compact tools rather than buying the biggest equipment available.
quotes.key_insight: Smart tool choices that prioritize the right attributes — blade quality over saw size, portability over raw power, value over brand prestige — can deliver outsized results in a home workshop.

quotes.quote: These little metal instruments of hyperaccurate joyous delight are an absolute staple for machinists; they wouldn't be caught dead without one.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro evangelizes calipers as an essential but overlooked tool for woodworkers, explaining their three measurement capabilities: thickness (pinching), width (interior measurement), and depth (via a sliding protrusion). He argues that while calipers are standard equipment for machinists and engineers, many woodworkers miss out on their hyper-accurate measuring abilities. The post reviews different caliper types—digital, fractional dial, and decimal dial—recommending decimal dial for experienced users and fractional dial for beginners. He advises against slide and vernier calipers as unnecessarily difficult when better alternatives are cheap.
quotes.key_insight: Calipers are a cheap, versatile precision measuring tool that every woodworker should own but most don't know about.

quotes.quote: From time to time, various objects in your shop may decide to jump up in the air and move in a straight line at very high speed.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews a $25 leather shop apron from woodworker.com, rating it 5/5 thumbs up. He argues that a leather apron is essential safety equipment for woodworkers because shop debris can fly at high speed and impale skin. The apron meets his requirements for coverage, leather construction, double-stitched seams, handy pockets, and easy on/off use. Despite some rough workmanship and lighter leather than a farrier's apron, he considers it a no-brainer purchase at the price point. The review is part of his 'NSY Free Tools' series, and includes a giveaway.
quotes.key_insight: Smart workshop safety gear doesn't need to be expensive — a $25 leather apron with useful pockets creates both physical protection and a behavioral incentive to actually wear it.

quotes.quote: You may think that's because there's a high demand among readers; that our discerning eye can reveal the finer points of differentiation between identical-seeming products. Well, you'd be wrong. That's not why woodworking writers get to write reviews.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro addresses the conflict of interest inherent in product reviews by woodworking writers who receive free tools from vendors. Rather than pretending the conflict doesn't exist, he devises a transparent policy: accept free tools, give one away to readers, write honest reviews but simply skip reviewing tools that are bad. He frames this as a mutually beneficial 'sellout' arrangement where both writer and readers benefit from free equipment while maintaining a baseline of honesty.
quotes.key_insight: Transparency about conflicts of interest — and sharing the spoils with your audience — is a more honest approach than pretending reviewer objectivity exists.

quotes.quote: It's because they get to keep the free stuff when they're done.

He frames this as a mutually beneficial 'sellout' arrangement where both writer and readers benefit from free equipment while maintaining a baseline of honesty.
quotes.key_insight: Transparency about conflicts of interest — and sharing the spoils with your audience — is a more honest approach than pretending reviewer objectivity exists.

quotes.quote: So I was all ready to proceed with my career as a sellout woodworking journalist loaded with free stuff, when I hit upon a brilliant fourth option: sell out, but make you sell out with me.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro addresses the conflict of interest inherent in product reviews by woodworking writers who receive free tools from vendors. Rather than pretending the conflict doesn't exist, he devises a transparent policy: accept free tools, give one away to readers, write honest reviews but simply skip reviewing tools that are bad. He frames this as a mutually beneficial 'sellout' arrangement where both writer and readers benefit from free equipment while maintaining a baseline of honesty.
quotes.key_insight: Transparency about conflicts of interest — and sharing the spoils with your audience — is a more honest approach than pretending reviewer objectivity exists.

quotes.quote: If I say it's great, it's great. I won't even leave out the bad parts. I just won't write reviews of crappy tools.

quotes.quote: If their finely tuned electrical system detects that the blade has contacted a finger–or a Jenny O hotdog–it brings the whole thing to a screeching halt.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro examines SawStop, a safety technology that can stop a tablesaw blade instantly upon detecting contact with skin. Despite his enthusiasm for the invention, he notes several practical barriers: no major saw manufacturer has agreed to install the system, forcing SawStop to sell their own custom saws. The technology is expensive, adding significant cost to already pricey equipment, and false alarms can be costly since the braking process destroys both the brake cartridge and the blade. Shapiro concludes he loves the idea but recommends caution until major manufacturers adopt it.
quotes.key_insight: Even brilliant safety innovations face an uphill battle when incumbent manufacturers won't adopt them, forcing inventors to become manufacturers themselves at a cost and reliability disadvantage.

quotes.quote: Sawstop is involved in a fascinating battle to get this technology to you, but so far, they haven't got a single saw manufacturer to install their system on their product line.

quotes.quote: There are two kinds of woodworkers. The first kind is often called the safety nut. The second kind is often called stubby.
quotes.summary: Shapiro offers practical tablesaw safety advice through his characteristic humor. He divides woodworkers into two categories — 'safety nuts' who use proper guards and pushsticks, and those called 'stubby' (implying they've lost fingers). The post focuses on a commonly overlooked danger: moving a slightly angled tablesaw fence to the opposite side of the blade, which reverses the tilt and causes dangerous kickback. It's a short, punchy safety reminder from his early blogging days about maker culture.
quotes.key_insight: Safety shortcuts in woodworking — and by extension any hands-on craft — have consequences that range from ruined work to serious injury, making attention to seemingly minor details like fence alignment essential.

quotes.quote: If you do, it will be leaning TOWARDS the blade, which absolutely guarantees that the piece will bind between the blade and the fence, and inevitably cause both burning of the piece and rapid projectile motion of your lumber towards your belly button, aka kickback.

quotes.quote: Why spend your own hard-earned dollars twice… your friendly federal government has spent the money already!
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recommends OSHA's free online woodworking safety resources as a practical guide for hobbyist woodworkers. He points out that since taxpayers already funded these materials, they might as well use them. The post specifically highlights OSHA's woodworking etool as an accessible, machine-by-machine safety guide. He contrasts the readable etool with OSHA's notoriously dense full regulatory codes.
quotes.key_insight: Taxpayer-funded OSHA woodworking safety resources are an underused, accessible free alternative to paid safety courses for hobbyist woodworkers.

quotes.quote: These are the guys whose job it is to monitor real, prodction woodworkers, figure out what's chopping off their fingers, and determine what to do with it.

quotes.quote: The trick is to tighten the pads down so the biesmeyer fence just barely slides over the guide rail.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical tip for calibrating a Biesmeyer-style rip fence on a table saw. After initial setup difficulties, he discovered that the far end of the fence wasn't staying true when reclamped. The fix was tightening the pads so the fence just barely slides over the guide rail, resulting in truer, squarer cuts.
quotes.key_insight: A Biesmeyer rip fence runs truer when its pads are tightened so it just barely slides over the guide rail, compensating for the single-clamp design.

quotes.quote: If you do this, the whole fence runs truer, stays squarer, and generally makes you happier.

quotes.quote: The sawdust is orange and should be treated like radioactive waste.
quotes.summary: Shapiro shares his experience working with cocobolo, an exotic hardwood with remarkable and unusual properties. The wood changes color dramatically — from red-orange to bright yellow after sanding, then back again when exposed to moisture. While beautiful and extremely durable, cocobolo produces allergenic sawdust that acts as a sensitizer, making repeated exposure increasingly dangerous. The post serves as both an enthusiastic recommendation and a practical safety warning for woodworkers considering this material.
quotes.key_insight: Cocobolo is a stunning and practically indestructible exotic wood whose remarkable properties come paired with equally remarkable hazards, demanding serious safety precautions.

quotes.quote: What makes a perfect jig? Well, here are my criteria: 1) Easy to make. If it ain't, I won't.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro describes building a simple jig for cutting perfect 45-degree miter joints for picture frames. After struggling with a more complex crosscut sled approach, he realized he could achieve the same result with just two pieces of scrap wood, one measurement, and two screws. The jig works by clamping material against a hardwood stick mounted at 45 degrees on plywood, which rides against the table saw fence. The post exemplifies his maker philosophy of favoring simplicity over complexity.
quotes.key_insight: The best workshop solutions come from simplifying the problem rather than engineering a more complex tool.

quotes.quote: Ever had a project that looked so good, you just wanted to lick it?
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro provides a practical guide to food-safe wood finishes for woodworkers who need their projects to be safe for contact with food or mouths. He ranks several finishing options from most to least durable, including shellac, salad bowl finish, pure tung oil, walnut oil, mineral oil, and other plant oils. The post notes important caveats like the difference between pure tung oil and commercial 'tung oil finish' products that contain heavy metals. He encourages woodworkers to turn scrap wood into functional items like butcher blocks, building blocks, and salad bowls using these safe alternatives.
quotes.key_insight: Woodworkers making food-contact items should choose from a specific set of food-safe finishes ranked by durability, from shellac down to mineral oil, rather than relying on potentially toxic commercial products.

quotes.quote: The single instrument that is most responsible for me Not Severing Anything Yet.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he's selling his trusty table saw on eBay after upgrading to a new one. He notes the current bid is surprisingly low compared to identical saws listed elsewhere. He sweetens the deal for blog readers by offering free spalted maple lumber to the winning bidder, with the condition they share photos of what they make and keep all fingers intact.
quotes.key_insight: A lighthearted woodworking post that showcases Shapiro's maker roots and community-minded approach to his craft long before his tech career milestones.

quotes.quote: If I mess 'em up, who cares–they're just going to get kicked anyway.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his personal method for applying a shellac finish to wood, specifically walnut table legs. The post is a detailed step-by-step guide covering sanding, pore-filling with pumice, and building up layers of shellac to achieve a smooth, glossy finish. He approaches the process with a casual, experimental attitude, treating shellac preparation like a cooking project. The technique involves packing pumice into wood pores with shellac, cleaning up with denatured alcohol, then progressively sanding with mineral oil before applying final coats.
quotes.key_insight: A high-quality shellac finish on open-grained wood comes from packing the pores with pumice and shellac before building up surface coats, and the process rewards a relaxed, experimental attitude over perfectionism.

quotes.quote: I treat shellac prep like a cooking project–that is to say cavalierly.

quotes.key_insight: A high-quality shellac finish on open-grained wood comes from packing the pores with pumice and shellac before building up surface coats, and the process rewards a relaxed, experimental attitude over perfectionism.

quotes.quote: Get a cabinet saw, not a contractor-style saw. Heavier trunnions mean less vibration.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his research on table saws, providing practical buying advice for woodworkers shopping under $1,500. He outlines three key criteria: cabinet-style construction over contractor-style, left-tilt blade orientation, and solid cast-iron wings. After evaluating three saws meeting these criteria, he recommends the Grizzly G1023SL as the clear winner based on features, reviews, and price.
quotes.key_insight: When buying a table saw, prioritize cabinet construction, left-tilt orientation, and cast-iron wings — and the best value meeting all three criteria is often not the most expensive option.

quotes.quote: Get a left-tilt saw. Most people put the fence on the right, and a right-tilt blade forces the cutoff down and into the fence. This makes kickback more likely, which makes everything more dangerous.

quotes.quote: I haven't shared the ultimate secret to fabulous finishes, not to mention saving a bundle on sandpaper: mineral oil wet sanding.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a woodworking tip about using mineral oil for wet sanding, which produces smoother finishes and dramatically extends sandpaper life. The post is structured as a humorous how-to guide, with the central comedic bit being the awkwardness of buying mineral oil (sold as a laxative) at the grocery store. He explains the technique: apply mineral oil liberally to the surface, then sand with 320 grit for results far superior to dry sanding. The post also touches on his broader sanding workflow, including pore-filling with pumice.
quotes.key_insight: Mineral oil from the pharmacy aisle is the cheapest and most effective wet-sanding lubricant for woodworking, producing superior finishes while making sandpaper last far longer.

quotes.quote: Humidity and wood are a treacherous combination.
quotes.summary: In this third installment of his humidor-building series, Shapiro explains the critical woodworking challenge of designing for humidity differentials. Since a humidor's interior maintains 70% relative humidity while the exterior faces much lower room humidity, the box experiences constant stress that demands careful joinery choices. He recommends strong glued joints (dovetails or fingerjoints) for the sides but slotted, unglued raised panels for the top and bottom to allow wood movement. He also shares his specific material choices and finishing tips, emphasizing that finishing must happen before assembly to preserve the panel's ability to move.
quotes.key_insight: Designing a humidor is fundamentally an exercise in engineering for constant wood movement caused by the humidity differential between interior and exterior.

quotes.quote: There is going to be incredible stress on the parts of your box, and you have to design for parts of the box to move.

quotes.quote: The key thing to remember is that you have to finish the top and bottom before you assemble it. Otherwise, you'll waste all your time with the raised-panel stuff, because the finish will lock everything in place, preventing movement.

quotes.quote: Basically, a humidor is a box within a box. The exterior box is something pretty, structurally sound, and moisture-proof.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro continues his series on building custom humidors, focusing on design principles. He explains the box-within-a-box construction where a pretty exterior surrounds a Spanish cedar interior, emphasizing the importance of a proper lip seal between lid and box. The post covers practical sizing considerations including length, height, width, and wall thickness, with specific measurements for accommodating various cigar sizes. He notes that humidors shouldn't be perfectly airtight since cigars release ammonia as they age. The post balances technical woodworking advice with humor, including a recommendation to use the golden ratio for aesthetic proportions.
quotes.key_insight: Good humidor design balances structural precision with practical forgiveness — the seal should be snug but not airtight, and the dimensions generous rather than tight.

quotes.quote: You don't actually want the thing hard-sealed, by the way; sure, it'd hold its humidity better, but cigars release a tiny bit of ammonia as they age, so a little air exchange is necessary or your cigars will pick up that distinctive eau de micturation.

quotes.quote: Shellac is too soft. You might manage it if you use natural (not blonde) shellac that's freshly mixed, but even then, I think you'd dent it with your dominoes.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro responds to a reader's question about choosing the right clear finish for a custom domino game table. The reader wants to embed pictures and memorabilia under a clear coat, similar to bar tops, and needs a durable, non-yellowing surface that can withstand domino play and spilled drinks. Shapiro evaluates several finish options from his woodworking experience, including shellac, epoxy, water-based varnishes, and Behlen's Rock Hard Tabletop Varnish. He ultimately recommends using a thick sheet of glass fused to the table with epoxy underneath, combining maximum durability with clarity.
quotes.key_insight: For maximum durability on a game table with embedded memorabilia, a thick glass top fused with epoxy outperforms any standalone finish by combining clarity, impact resistance, and protection.

quotes.quote: I have experimented with exactly the goop you link to above, and it's tough stuff. You'll probably dent it and scuff it, but not break it.

quotes.quote: I was generating HUGE quantities of sawdust, so I figured I must be making progress.
quotes.summary: Shapiro recounts a woodworking mistake where he tried to sand down a too-thick humidor part using 400-grit sandpaper instead of low-grit. Despite producing enormous quantities of sawdust that gave the illusion of progress, he spent an hour making almost no real headway. The high grit number means more cutting surfaces producing many tiny particles—volume of sawdust without meaningful material removal. It's a simple lesson about using the right tool for the job and not confusing the appearance of productivity with actual progress.
quotes.key_insight: Visible busyness and output can be deeply misleading—generating huge quantities of sawdust doesn't mean you're actually removing wood, and the appearance of productivity is not the same as real progress.

quotes.quote: Higher grit means more cutting surfaces means more particles of sawdust… they're just really small particles.

quotes.quote: I tried old T-shirts, socks, just about everything, but kept having problems with lint and pilling, which would show up in the finish.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical tip from his woodworking experience: microfiber towels work excellently for applying shellac and other finishes. After struggling with lint and pilling from various cloth materials while finishing a coffee table, he discovered microfiber towels at Costco. He tested them on a humidor with great results, noting only that colored microfiber can leach dye into alcohol-based finishes.
quotes.key_insight: Microfiber towels are a superior alternative to common household fabrics for applying shellac and other finishes, as they eliminate lint and pilling problems.

quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical tip from his woodworking experience: microfiber towels work excellently for applying shellac and other finishes. After struggling with lint and pilling from various cloth materials while finishing a coffee table, he discovered microfiber towels at Costco. He tested them on a humidor with great results, noting only that colored microfiber can leach dye into alcohol-based finishes.
quotes.key_insight: Microfiber towels are a superior alternative to common household fabrics for applying shellac and other finishes, as they eliminate lint and pilling problems.

quotes.quote: I divide tool brands into two categories. These are, roughly, 'cheap and crappy' and 'good and expensive'.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro categorizes power tool brands into two tiers: 'Good and Expensive' (G&E) and 'Cheap and Crappy' (C&C). G&E brands like DeWalt, Milwaukee, and Porter Cable reliably deliver quality at their normal price points, but can disappoint when they slap their name on cheap products. C&C brands are defined not by being universally bad, but by their inconsistency—some products are excellent while others are junk. The key takeaway is that G&E brands let you buy with confidence, while C&C brands require careful research before purchasing.
quotes.key_insight: Premium tool brands buy you the freedom to skip research, while budget brands demand careful per-product evaluation because their defining trait is inconsistency, not universal poor quality.

quotes.quote: If it ain't expensive, you shouldn't count on it being good either, even if it's from a 'Good and Expensive' brand.

quotes.quote: The bad boy makes sawdust like a woodchuck.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro follows up on a previous router bit review post, reporting his experience with an Oldham Viper 3/4 straight bit. He used it on walnut and maple for trimming and rabbeting tasks. He found it cuts aggressively and produces a noticeably better finish than cheaper carbide bits. He recommends readers pick some up.
quotes.key_insight: Investing in a quality router bit like the Oldham Viper delivers noticeably better performance and finish than budget carbide alternatives.

quotes.quote: When gluing up something that will be hard to clean when complete, use masking tape to keep the squeezeout from messing up your project.
quotes.summary: A brief woodworking tip about using masking tape on inside corners before gluing to prevent messy squeeze-out. Shapiro recommends masking off hard-to-clean areas, letting the glue set for 30-45 minutes, then peeling off the tape for clean results. The post is a quick practical maker tip with no broader argument.
quotes.key_insight: Prevent glue mess on hard-to-reach joints by masking off corners with tape before assembly.

quotes.quote: It's the decay left behind by a specific type of fungus.
quotes.summary: This short post explains the origin of spalted wood's distinctive patterns — they come from fungal decay. Shapiro advises woodworkers that spalted wood must be harvested at the right moment before it loses structural integrity, and that the fungal spores remain on the wood after harvesting. He recommends either kiln-drying, oven-sterilizing at 130 degrees for eight hours, or sealing with shellac to kill or contain the spores. The post is a concise, practical tip for woodworkers buying or processing their own spalted lumber.
quotes.key_insight: Spalted wood's beauty comes from fungal decay, and woodworkers must sterilize or seal it to prevent the spores from causing further damage.

quotes.quote: The wood must be harvested when the spalting is pronounced and visible, but wait too long and the wood loses its structural integrity.

quotes.quote: Miter slots stink. No two ways about it, it's really hard to make a tool that works well in a miter slot.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro explains why standard miter slots on tablesaws are frustratingly imprecise, causing inaccurate cuts. He shares a personal anecdote about making a picture frame that shrank to wallet-size after repeated corrections. He recommends either buying an expensive adjustable miter gauge or building a custom crosscut sled with runners machined to fit your specific table.
quotes.key_insight: Stock tablesaw miter gauges are too imprecise for accurate work; building a custom crosscut sled with runners machined to fit your specific table is the best solution.

quotes.quote: I actually made a whole damn picture frame before I figured out why I had to recut each side eighteen times to get it right on 45 degrees… the resulting frame held a wallet-size picture, but that's besides the point.

quotes.quote: Do the two lines overlap, or do they make a skinny "V"? Yeah, thought so.
quotes.summary: Shapiro demonstrates that most combination squares are inaccurate by describing a simple flip test that reveals whether the tool is truly square. He then presents three solutions at different price points: an expensive precision square, a dedicated fixed square, or a cheap rafter square made from a single piece of aluminum with no moving parts to fail. The post implicitly advocates for the simplest, most reliable tool over complex or expensive alternatives.
quotes.key_insight: The simplest tool with no moving parts often outperforms complex, expensive alternatives — reliability comes from eliminating failure modes, not adding features.

quotes.quote: It's just one solid piece of aluminum, no moving parts, nothing to mess up.

quotes.quote: NOT any other kind of cedar, or your cigars will smell like a hamster cage.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical advice on building a humidor as a woodworking project after ordering a large batch of cigars. He explains why humidors are ideal small projects that allow use of scrap wood and small quantities of expensive figured woods. The post focuses on sourcing Spanish cedar for the humidor lining, warning against substitutes and offering a cost-saving tip: getting free or cheap cedar cigar boxes from local cigar shops. He notes the irony of asking your local shop for help after buying cigars online.
quotes.key_insight: Resourceful makers can build quality humidors cheaply by recycling Spanish cedar from cigar boxes sourced from local shops.

quotes.quote: I took a cabinet scraper and ran it back and forth, shearing off a bit of the edge.
quotes.summary: Shapiro describes a practical woodworking technique for cleaning up dadoes (grooves) that were cut with a dull blade, leaving wood shreds and undersized joints. He recommends using a cabinet scraper run back and forth to shear off material and widen the dado. The technique requires two passes per side but is fast and effective. He notes the dadoes came from an ecabinets product, not his own work.
quotes.key_insight: A cabinet scraper is a fast, controlled way to clean up and widen undersized dadoes without rounding the joint shoulders.

quotes.quote: It's easy to keep at 90 degrees so you don't round over the shoulder, nice and wide so you can control it, and pretty quick.

quotes.quote: I was gluing up our dining room table and hit a snag–the longest pipe clamps I have were 4" too short.
quotes.summary: Shapiro shares a practical woodworking tip from building his own dining room table. When his pipe clamps were 4 inches too short, he realized that since pipe clamps are literally made of pipe, he could extend them using standard pipe couplers. He walks through the technique, noting the tradeoff of a bump at the coupler joint that can cause bowing, and offers workarounds like alternating clamp sides or using multiple short segments to distribute the bumps evenly.
quotes.key_insight: Pipe clamps are just pipes, so standard plumbing fittings like couplers can extend them when they're too short for a project.

quotes.quote: Well, fortunately I remembered that since they're made of PIPES, I can do all the usual pipe-y stuff to them, including put couplers on them.

quotes.quote: The catch, as always, is in the fundamental zen nature of the glue itself: it's supposed to stick to things.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro compares two methods for cleaning up wood glue squeeze-out after clamping: chipping hardened glue with a chisel versus wiping it while still wet. He explains that chipping risks damaging the wood surface, while naive wiping leaves a thin glue haze that ruins staining. The breakthrough insight, shared by a friend, is to use boiling water from an electric kettle, which dramatically improves the wipe method's effectiveness due to increased solubility at higher temperatures. He recommends this hot-water approach for hardwoods, with caveats about burn safety and timing.
quotes.key_insight: Using boiling water instead of cold water transforms glue cleanup from a frustrating compromise into a reliable technique, thanks to the basic chemistry of temperature-dependent solubility.

quotes.quote: When you pry it off, it has a tendancy to drag small chunks and splinters of your newly completed furniture along with it, kicking and screaming.

quotes.quote: In my experience, a purchase like this often results in a 'slide to the median'.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro comments on Black & Decker's acquisition of Pentair's tool group, which includes respected brands Delta and Porter-Cable. He speculates that the merger will result in a 'slide to the median' where the acquired brands may gain access to cutting-edge innovations but lose some quality as cost-trimming and parts-sharing take effect. He notes that B&D's own quality may improve from the closer association. He concludes there are no short-term impacts on tool buying decisions.
quotes.key_insight: When a mass-market company acquires premium brands, both tend to converge toward mediocrity — the acquired brands lose quality while gaining mainstream innovations.

quotes.quote: Their quality may also sag a bit as they start trying to trim inefficiencies, parts sharing with B&D for example.

quotes.quote: Don't use spalted maple; the fungus that makes the pretty spalting is toxic.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro follows up on a previous butcher block woodworking project, reporting that the tung oil finish worked well and the block survived heavy use during a large dinner party. He provides five practical recommendations for anyone making their own butcher block, covering wood selection, end-grain construction, finishing techniques, sizing for planers, and safety. The post reflects his hands-on maker enthusiasm, presenting the project as an accessible and rewarding woodworking endeavor that makes great gifts.
quotes.key_insight: Building an end-grain butcher block is a rewarding, accessible woodworking project that yields a durable, functional kitchen tool when you choose the right wood and finish.

quotes.quote: I might learn something about Korean woodworking and techniques; maybe even pick up a distinctly Korean tool and a few pieces of interesting wood to stash in the carry-on.
quotes.summary: Shapiro recounts a brief trip to Seoul, South Korea, where he tried and failed to find a traditional woodworking store to buy chisels. He had hoped to find Japanese-style chisels at a lower price or discover distinctly Korean woodworking tools and techniques. Instead, he was repeatedly directed to small power-tool kiosks selling brands like Bosch. He closes by asking readers if anyone knows about Korean woodworking traditions.
quotes.key_insight: A lighthearted travel anecdote about the difficulty of finding traditional craft supplies in a modernized city, reflecting Shapiro's early interest in hands-on making.

quotes.quote: More expensive bits cut better. Saywhat?
quotes.summary: Shapiro expresses frustration with a woodworking magazine's unhelpful router bit review that offered no specific data or rankings, only the vague conclusion that expensive bits cut better. He then shares a useful review from Fine Woodworker that includes actual comparative data. The post highlights that mid-priced Oldham Viper bits from Home Depot performed well, while some expensive brands ranked near the bottom. It's a brief, practical maker-community post from early in Shapiro's blogging history.
quotes.key_insight: Good product reviews require actual data and rankings, not just the lazy conclusion that you get what you pay for.

quotes.quote: As soon as the blade hit the wood fibers I was brutally reminded that my chisels are absolute, unmitigated, crap.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro documents his quest to buy decent woodworking chisels after realizing his current set is worthless. After using a friend's mediocre-but-functional Irwin/Marples chisels, he's reminded that his own chisels are even worse—especially after his wife used them to chip cement. He sets a $60 budget and evaluates four options ranging from cheap sets with wine money left over to two premium Japanese chisels rated #1 by Fine Woodworking.
quotes.key_insight: When buying tools on a budget, the core decision is whether to get more adequate tools or fewer excellent ones—a tradeoff that applies well beyond woodworking.

quotes.quote: Frank built a 6' long rotisserie out of a couple of bicycles, a washing machine motor, and some stainless steel.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a project by his friend Frank who built a 6-foot-long rotisserie out of two bicycles, a washing machine motor, and stainless steel. They used it to cook 140 pounds of lamb for Shapiro's wedding. The post is a brief personal share with a link to Frank's project page, notable mainly as an early example of Shapiro's interest in maker culture and creative engineering.
quotes.key_insight: Creative engineering with repurposed everyday objects can solve surprisingly large-scale problems like catering a wedding.

quotes.quote: The good folks at Feedburner are troubleshooting the missing newlines in the new feed.
quotes.summary: This is a very brief technical update post from 2004 where Dan Shapiro notes that Feedburner is troubleshooting missing newlines in his blog's new RSS feed. The post is essentially a status update to readers experiencing feed issues. It thanks readers for their patience and points them to subscription options. There is no substantive argument or analysis in this post.
quotes.key_insight: This is a minor technical status update about RSS feed formatting issues, with no substantive content to analyze.

quotes.quote: Read until it stops making sense or you stop caring.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he has switched his blog's RSS syndication to Feedburner's smartfeed system. He explains the change at escalating levels of technical detail, from 'nothing changed' for casual readers to redirect specifics for RSS power users. The post is a practical tech update wrapped in humor, advising readers to point their newsreaders at his canonical URL rather than the Feedburner URL directly, so he can revert easily if needed. It's a snapshot of mid-2000s blogging infrastructure concerns.
quotes.key_insight: A lighthearted 2004 blog housekeeping post about RSS feed migration that captures the era's blogging culture and infrastructure concerns.

quotes.quote: Wood glue joints are typically stronger than the wood itself. So why bother strengthening the joint?
quotes.summary: Shapiro evaluates four wood joinery methods—plain glue, biscuits, dowels, and splines—for gluing up hardwood boards into flat tabletops. He grades each on strength, ease of use, cost, invisibility, and most importantly alignment. After extensive hands-on testing, he concludes that dowels with a high-quality jig (the Dowelmax) deliver the best results, particularly for alignment. The post is bookended by a lengthy, darkly comic account of a woodworking injury involving a lost fingernail and a cavalier hand surgeon.
quotes.key_insight: For gluing up hardwood tabletops, alignment matters more than joint strength, and a precision dowel jig outperforms biscuits and plain glue on the metric that actually counts.

quotes.quote: The point of a strong joint isn't actually the joint, but rather the areas near the joint.

quotes.quote: When I asked the doctor if I was going to lose the nail, he laughed at me.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts a painful workshop injury sustained while building a dining room table. While tightening pipe clamps on a glue-up, a crescent wrench slipped and slammed his fingernail into a neighboring clamp. He had to finish clamping before the glue dried, then visited a doctor who laughed at his question about losing the nail. The post serves as a cautionary tale that hand tools can be just as dangerous as power tools.
quotes.key_insight: Hand tools deserve the same respect as power tools — complacency around seemingly safe equipment can still lead to serious injury.

quotes.quote: Damn, those carbide tips are HUGE.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his real-time impressions of receiving and testing a Forrester Woodworker II table saw blade. He documents the experience chronologically from unboxing through first cuts, noting the excessive grease on the blade, its impressively quiet operation, and its excellent performance cutting walnut. While the blade doesn't leave quite as mirror-smooth a finish as his previous 80-tooth Freud blade, he concludes it's a delightful tool that meets expectations even if it falls slightly short of the hype.
quotes.key_insight: The Forrester Woodworker II is an excellent ripping blade that trades finish quality for quiet operation and powerful cutting, making it a strong but not miraculous upgrade for serious woodworkers.
