quotes.quote: But woe betide the one who doesn't inspect it adequately first, or who doesn't read the description carefully!
quotes.summary: In this follow-up post, Shapiro recounts his friend's experience at a Grizzly woodworking tool tent sale, where apparent bargains require careful inspection. His friend bought a seemingly great deal on a longbed jointer marked down from $800 to $500, only to discover it was missing blades, a pulley, and had a cracked cast-iron base — adding $200 in parts back to the cost. The post serves as a cautionary tale about reading defect descriptions carefully and thoroughly inspecting discounted machinery before purchasing. Shapiro advises future tent sale shoppers to print parts diagrams ahead of time for proper checkout.
quotes.key_insight: A bargain on used tools is only a bargain if you inspect thoroughly enough to understand what you're actually getting — ambiguous defect descriptions can hide deal-breaking problems.

quotes.quote: The 'cracked base' that the description mentioned referred not to the sheet-metal stand, but rather the actual cast-iron jointer base.

quotes.quote: The tent sale is their chance to unload the planer that rusted on the way from Shanghai, the tablesaw that has minor bloodstains from the troublesome Safety-Shield demo, and everything else that can't be sold through normal channels.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts a trip to the Grizzly woodworking tool tent sale with a friend, describing the bargain-hunting culture around discounted industrial machinery. He explains that Grizzly imports Chinese tools at low prices with American motors and their own QA, and the tent sale is where they unload damaged or unsellable inventory at even steeper discounts. The post captures the frenzy of early-bird shoppers who camp out for the best deals, and shares a tactical tip about arriving at 10 AM to catch re-tagged items that first-round buyers abandoned. It's a lighthearted slice-of-life piece from Shapiro's woodworking hobby days, well before his later ventures in tech and AI.
quotes.key_insight: The best bargains at competitive sales often go not to the earliest arrivals but to those who understand the system's second-chance mechanics.

quotes.quote: Brave souls line up at 8 in the morning, even camping out the night before, and charge in first thing in a fit of scavenging fury.

quotes.quote: They burst forth with literally tons of machinery, and the checkout/loading line can take an hour or more.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts a trip to the Grizzly woodworking tool tent sale with a friend, describing the bargain-hunting culture around discounted industrial machinery. He explains that Grizzly imports Chinese tools at low prices with American motors and their own QA, and the tent sale is where they unload damaged or unsellable inventory at even steeper discounts. The post captures the frenzy of early-bird shoppers who camp out for the best deals, and shares a tactical tip about arriving at 10 AM to catch re-tagged items that first-round buyers abandoned. It's a lighthearted slice-of-life piece from Shapiro's woodworking hobby days, well before his later ventures in tech and AI.
quotes.key_insight: The best bargains at competitive sales often go not to the earliest arrivals but to those who understand the system's second-chance mechanics.

quotes.quote: If someone tears off the tag from a machine but doesn't pay for it, after two hours, it gets re-tagged and you can buy it again.

quotes.quote: According to the reviews (all 63 of them!) this is *the* best blade for just about any woodworking task.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his excitement about acquiring a Forrest Woodworker II table saw blade, obtained through a barter arrangement with a co-worker who used his workshop. He notes the blade's overwhelmingly positive reviews and plans to use it for his next project, a dining room table. He expresses curiosity about its claimed versatility with plywood despite having fewer teeth than typical sheet goods blades.
quotes.key_insight: A short, excited post about acquiring a well-regarded woodworking blade, reflecting Shapiro's hands-on maker sensibility and willingness to barter shop access for quality tools.

quotes.quote: I'm curious about it's claims to be great on plywood too–usually sheet goods blades are 60+ tooth

quotes.quote: That's what I want, a fish-it-yourself restaurant.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts a dinner at Palisade, a Seattle seafood restaurant he recommends for tourists. He describes the restaurant's panoramic waterfront views, live fish displays, and excellent seafood. The core of the post is his discovery that the restaurant's extensive woodwork is made entirely of Koa, an expensive Hawaiian hardwood he had coincidentally just read about. He jokes about wanting a fish-it-yourself restaurant and closes with affectionate asides about his mother-in-law and his own small stash of Koa wood.
quotes.key_insight: An early personal blog post revealing Shapiro's eye for craftsmanship and materials — interests that would later manifest in his founding of Glowforge.

quotes.quote: Your local forest, stream, creek, or spotted owl nest will thank me for it
quotes.summary: A very brief post from 2004 where Dan Shapiro promotes the Rechargeable Battery Recycling Corporation (RBRC) as a resource for recycling cordless power tool batteries. He encourages readers to recycle their batteries to prevent heavy metals from entering the environment, with a lighthearted nod to local wildlife benefiting from the effort.
quotes.key_insight: Responsible disposal of power tool batteries through dedicated recycling programs helps keep heavy metals out of local ecosystems.

quotes.quote: A magazine that you probably haven't read produces a site that you probably haven't found.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recommends a niche woodworking website's 'Wood of the Month' feature from a trade magazine he discovered at a tool sale. He notes the magazine is narrowly targeted at high-volume professional woodworkers and mostly not relevant to him, but finds the wood profiles interesting. He also mentions the publisher offers free subscriptions to subtly different magazine variants for different woodworking specialties.
quotes.key_insight: An early glimpse into Shapiro's maker culture interests, sharing a niche woodworking resource years before founding laser-cutter company Glowforge.

quotes.quote: They don't want to let you sign up for all of them, probably because they're all quite similar.

quotes.quote: If you compare to just about any other retail source, this is the cheapest you can find plywood with two presentable sides.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical tip about affordable plywood available at Home Depot. He describes maple/birch 4x8 sheets available for around $35, noting their limitations but emphasizing their value compared to other retail sources. He recommends the plywood for shop use, jigs, and cabinet backs, and notes the convenience of Home Depot's cutting service.
quotes.key_insight: Affordable maple/birch plywood at Home Depot offers hobbyist woodworkers a practical material with two presentable sides at a price point well below other retail sources.

quotes.quote: Buy a sheet for your next project. You'll thank me for it.

quotes.quote: Part IV in a 3-or-so part post describing how I made our coffee table.
quotes.summary: This is the fourth and final installment in a series about building a custom slate-and-wood coffee table. The post is essentially a photo showcase of the completed project, presenting corner detail and isometric views of the finished piece. Shapiro invites readers who are inspired to try similar projects to share their results, and offers to provide more detailed instructions to anyone attempting the build. It's a brief, celebratory wrap-up to a multi-part DIY furniture project.
quotes.key_insight: A hands-on DIY furniture project wraps up with pride in the finished product and an invitation for others to try building their own.

quotes.quote: Once this dries up, nothing short of a flaming jackhammer will cause your table distress.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro details the final construction steps of a custom coffee table with inlaid slate tiles. The post covers leg construction from laminated 6/4 stock, attachment techniques using hidden screws beneath the slate, and slate tile preparation from inexpensive Home Depot tiles. The bulk of the article focuses on finishing with shellac, including dissolving flakes, pore-filling with pumice, and a simplified French polish technique. The post is written as a practical woodworking tutorial with humorous asides about bug excretions, potable alcohol, and using a red sock as a finishing pad.
quotes.key_insight: Building quality furniture is accessible with basic materials and techniques, and the imperfect, personality-filled process is as much the point as the finished product.

quotes.quote: Woodworkers call that side the 'bottom'.
quotes.summary: This is Part II of a multi-part series where Dan Shapiro documents building a custom walnut and slate coffee table. The post walks through the woodworking process from selecting and preparing lumber through gluing up the tabletop. He details a clever technique using dado cuts to create perfectly square recesses for slate tile inlays that look hand-chiseled but are actually done quickly on a table saw. The writing blends practical woodworking instruction with humor and personality, revealing Shapiro's deep enthusiasm for the craft.
quotes.key_insight: Clever use of power tools and technique can produce results that look like painstaking handcraft — the smartest woodworking is knowing which shortcuts are invisible.

quotes.quote: It'll look like you carefully chiseled out the opening with handtools and got 12 perfectly square corners, but you actually did the whole thing in an hour with your table saw.

quotes.quote: The profs who taught me proper GD&T will kick my ass when they see it.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro introduces a multi-part blog series documenting how he built a custom coffee table with a slate inset. He shares the blueprints for the project under a Creative Commons license, allowing others to build from his plans but not modify or sell them commercially. The post is largely an introduction and table of contents for the series, with self-deprecating humor about his technical drawings. He notes the plans were created in TurboCAD v8 and explains his licensing rationale based on past experiences with people reselling free work.
quotes.key_insight: Shapiro's early maker ethos is evident in openly sharing furniture blueprints while carefully protecting them with Creative Commons licensing — balancing generosity with hard-won lessons about free work being exploited.

quotes.quote: Still, it was good enough to print out, scatter copies of around the shop, spill shellac on, and ultimately produce a table from.

quotes.quote: As someone who's owned a planer for nearly four months and planed literally dozens of board-feet of lumber, I feel fully qualified to answer your question.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro answers a reader's question about why her planer keeps tearing chunks from the corners of boards during final thickness passes. He offers two theories: moisture absorption at board ends weakening the wood, and the possibility of heavily figured wood causing tearout. He provides practical solutions including using sacrificial scrap lumber, checking blade sharpness, and avoiding thick passes. The post showcases Shapiro's self-deprecating humor, admitting to only four months of planer ownership while confidently diagnosing the problem.
quotes.key_insight: Chunking at board ends during final planer passes likely results from moisture-weakened end grain or figured wood tearout, both solvable with sacrificial backing boards and sharp blades.

quotes.quote: By the time you're at the end of the board, you don't have half a tree of lumber behind you to hold the whole thing in place.

quotes.quote: Yup, I'm serious. I've got one, and you should too.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro enthusiastically recommends the Death Stick EX-9 'Exhumer,' a cat's paw style nail puller tool. He describes how the tool works by digging prongs under a nail head and leveraging it out. The post is a brief, lighthearted product recommendation that highlights the tool's cool name and built-in bottle opener as bonus features.
quotes.key_insight: A brief, playful tool recommendation showing Shapiro's maker culture roots and appreciation for well-branded physical products.

quotes.quote: I suppose there's no better way to learn than to try it out.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his excitement about purchasing a Jet 12-inch bandsaw along with Timberwolf blades and an upgraded fence. He explains the key technical considerations for bandsaw blades—thickness for tracking straight lines versus cutting curves, and tooth set width for turning ease versus minimizing wood waste. The post is a brief maker's journal entry about tooling up for a planned Japanese-styled table with tricky curves.
quotes.key_insight: An early glimpse of Shapiro's maker mindset—diving into unfamiliar tools and learning through ambitious hands-on projects—years before founding Glowforge.

quotes.key_insight: An early glimpse of Shapiro's maker mindset—diving into unfamiliar tools and learning through ambitious hands-on projects—years before founding Glowforge.

quotes.quote: Clearly, they're not marking dirty bits and only doing the necessary work; they're rebuilding the whole shmeer each time anything might have been changed.
quotes.summary: Shapiro documents ongoing frustrations with eCabinets software, a cabinet design tool he's been evaluating. He catalogs four major issues: frequent crashes tied to the render engine, poor performance from naive rebuild-everything architecture, alarming bugs that corrupt files, and missing features like closed box construction. Despite the litany of problems, he notes the vendor is responsive with fixes and concludes the benefits still outweigh the drawbacks.
quotes.key_insight: Shipping buggy software is forgivable if you fix bugs fast, but easily reproducible corruption bugs suggest a lack of any real testing methodology.

quotes.quote: This is unnerving because it's easy to reproduce, and makes me think they may not have a robust testing methadology. Or any testing methadology.

quotes.quote: The sawblade was wandering around the lumber like a drunken fratboy on mardi gras.
quotes.summary: Shapiro recounts his experience with a cheap Ryobi bandsaw that proved useless for cutting curved pieces from thick lumber but turned out to be perfect for trimming splines on picture frames. The blade wandered too much for precision work on 8/4 stock but handled the delicate spline-trimming task cleanly. He shares a practical tip from a friend about starting flush-cut saw cuts from the loose end to avoid breaking spline corners. The post is a lighthearted reminder that the right tool for the job isn't always the best tool — sometimes a junky tool finds its niche.
quotes.key_insight: An imperfect tool isn't a useless tool — matching the right task to the right (even crappy) equipment can yield surprisingly good results.

quotes.quote: I ran the only-slightly-bloodied edge of that old picture frame along the junky bandsaw blade, and it was a match made in heaven.

quotes.quote: Not the curve, but the slice o'contrasting beauty you stick in the corner of something to strengthen it.
quotes.summary: Shapiro describes his experience cutting splines — thin strips of contrasting wood used to strengthen picture frame corners. He walks through the practical challenges of cutting thin strips on a table saw safely, particularly the kickback danger of placing the thin piece between the blade and fence. After wasting wood on multiple failed attempts, he shares a workaround: cutting the spline groove but not all the way through, then finishing with a hand saw and chisel to avoid repeated fence adjustments.
quotes.key_insight: In woodworking, the safest and most repeatable approach often means abandoning the obvious power-tool method in favor of a hybrid technique that trades speed for precision and safety.

quotes.quote: The obvious way to do that is put the fence 1/8 of an inch away from the sawblade, and that's an excellent recipe for turning your spline into a projectile weapon.

quotes.quote: It does still have bad problems with crash-prone-ness, but saving frequently solves most of that.
quotes.summary: Shapiro continues his review of eCabinet Systems software, documenting his experience working through its extensive 350+ page tutorial. While impressed by the system's flexibility for designing cabinets with sheet goods, he identifies a practical gap: the software doesn't adequately address how to finish exposed plywood edges. He notes ongoing crash issues but remains cautiously optimistic, withholding final judgment until he produces an actual cabinet with the tool.
quotes.key_insight: Design software is only as good as its ability to address real-world fabrication details — the gap between a nice digital model and a finished physical object is where tools prove their worth.

quotes.quote: You can buy just about everything cordless these days, but that doesn't mean you should.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro provides a comprehensive buyer's guide to cordless power tools, written for a non-expert audience. He recommends which tools to buy cordless (drill is essential, reciprocating saw is useful, circular saw is questionable), ranks major brands by quality and price, and shares strategies for getting the best deals. The core advice is to stick with one brand for battery compatibility, with Porter-Cable and Ryobi as top picks at different price points. He warns against formerly reputable brands like Black & Decker, Craftsman, and Ridgid that have declined in quality.
quotes.key_insight: When buying cordless tools, commit to one brand ecosystem for battery compatibility, and for most homeowners, budget brands like Ryobi deliver professional-grade durability at a fraction of the cost.

quotes.quote: A contractor I know put it to me this way: 'I can buy a Milwaukee and it'll last me 10 years, or I can buy a Ryobi for a quarter of the price and have it last 3 years.'

quotes.quote: Yeah, I realize I'm accumulating quite a backlog of projects. That's OK, designing is half the fun.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares inspiration images of Japanese step tansu chests from various furniture retailers, considering one for an upcoming woodworking or maker project. The brief post is essentially a mood board of tansu designs he finds appealing. He acknowledges his growing backlog of projects but embraces it, noting that the design phase is enjoyable in itself.
quotes.key_insight: For a dedicated maker, the design and inspiration phase is as rewarding as the build itself.

quotes.quote: Freeplans.com actually appears not to be a rip-off.
quotes.summary: Shapiro shares his discovery of Freeplans.com, a service that emails free woodworking plans supported by advertising. He reports being pleasantly surprised that the first plan he received was high-quality, with clear instructions and pictures for building a cherry bookcase. He also explains his privacy-conscious approach of using a disposable 'tearoff' email address to detect if the service sells his information.
quotes.key_insight: Even in the early web era, Shapiro's maker curiosity and tech-savvy privacy habits were already evident in how he approached free online resources.

quotes.quote: I used a 'tearoff' email address, so I'll know if they sell my name.

quotes.quote: It really is free; there appear to be no strings attached whatsoever.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews eCabinet Systems' free cabinet design software after spending a week learning it. He finds it genuinely free with no strings attached and easy to install, but plagued by bugs, crashes, and poor error handling. The UI is a mix of Windows-standard and AutoCAD-compatible conventions with questionable design choices, though he notes it's learnable. Despite the rough edges, he's enthusiastic about using it to design bookcases, and mentions his background as a lead program manager for the Windows XP UI at Microsoft.
quotes.key_insight: Even free, feature-rich software loses trust through poor error handling and UI inconsistencies — problems that dedicated testing and design attention could easily fix.

quotes.quote: Turns out that what I thought was the 'normal' part of sanding was actually me rubbing dead limp sandless paper repeatedly over wood.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a woodworking lesson about sandpaper replacement frequency. He discovered that sandpaper loses its effectiveness after just five to ten minutes of use, and that he had been sanding with essentially dead paper for most of his sessions. By changing sandpaper frequently, sanding tasks become dramatically faster and produce better finishes. The post is part of a broader theme about not skimping on disposable shop supplies.
quotes.key_insight: The hidden cost of skimping on disposable supplies is not the money saved but the vastly greater time wasted working with degraded tools.

quotes.quote: Depending on the quality of your sandpaper, you have five to ten minutes of serious sanding each time you change the pad.

quotes.quote: It fails gracefully. This is critical for me, since I fail often, and so must my tools.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews his used Ridgid TP-1300 planer, praising it as his favorite power tool. He evaluates it on five criteria: accurate performance, idiot-proof design, graceful failure modes, tolerance of operator error, and the sheer joy of revealing beautiful wood grain. The post doubles as a meditation on what makes a great tool — one that works as advertised, anticipates user mistakes, and delivers magical results. He recommends readers try planing mystery rough-cut lumber, while noting his planer is from the pre-decline Ridgid era.
quotes.key_insight: The best tools are ones that fail gracefully, tolerate imperfect users, and transform raw material into something revelatory.

quotes.quote: Nothing's for sure, but a heavy leather shop apron might have saved him.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a brief cautionary tale about table saw safety after reading about a fatal accident where a splinter kicked back and killed a woodworker. He advocates for wearing a heavy leather shop apron as a potentially life-saving piece of safety equipment. He notes the practical bonus of always having a tape measure handy. The post ends with him convincing a friend to go apron shopping after telling the story to the friend's wife.
quotes.key_insight: Simple, inexpensive safety gear like a leather shop apron can be the difference between a close call and a fatal workshop accident.

quotes.quote: Plus, you've always got your tape measure handy–beats the hell out of my current system of strategically placing tape measures every five feet around the shop.

quotes.quote: Not an ED ad, this is a serious subject for amateur sawdust-makers.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical advice for amateur woodworkers on sourcing cheap lumber directly from local sawyers rather than retail hardwood stores. He explains that prices can be less than half of retail, with the added benefits of matched boards from single trees and more interesting character wood. The post walks through the key consideration of moisture content, covering kiln-dried, air-dried, and wet wood scenarios. He warns that wet wood requires proper storage and carries risk of cracking and warping, and advises buyers to verify moisture claims with a meter rather than trusting the seller. The post concludes with a concrete example: 100 board feet of air-dried maple for $0.70/bf, cheaper than construction-grade 2x4s.
quotes.key_insight: Buying lumber directly from local sawyers can dramatically reduce costs for woodworkers, but requires understanding moisture content and drying methods to avoid expensive mistakes.

quotes.quote: Evidently it lets you design your cabinets in a CAD environment, then shows you renderings of the finished cabinets, lets you order all the hardware wholesale, produces cutlists, and ultimately creates files you can send to have the wood milled for you.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his excitement about ordering eCabinet Systems, a CAD software for cabinet design recommended by his woodworker neighbor. The software handles the full workflow from design to manufacturing: CAD modeling, rendered previews, wholesale hardware ordering, cut lists, and CNC mill-ready files. He plans to post a follow-up review once he's used it.
quotes.key_insight: An early glimpse of Shapiro's longstanding interest in software that bridges digital design and physical fabrication — a thread that would eventually lead to Glowforge.

quotes.quote: Don't skimp; buy the right tools for the job.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts accidentally cutting his thumb with a box knife while trimming picture frame splines, resulting in a trip to the ER and five stitches. The incident happened because he was using a knife instead of the proper flush cut saw he'd been too cheap to buy. He shares three lessons learned: always consider where the knife will go if it slips, invest in the right tools instead of improvising, and bring a book to the ER. He publicly shares the embarrassing story hoping it will make him more careful in the future.
quotes.key_insight: Skimping on the right tools is a false economy that can cost you far more than what you saved.

quotes.quote: If you need a big pack of sandpaper in a hurry, get it from Lowe's, not Home Depot. You'll thank me for it.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a quick price comparison for hook-and-loop sandpaper pads between Lowe's and Home Depot, finding Lowe's offers twice the quantity for the same price with a better brand (Porter Cable vs. Norton). He notes both retailers have poor websites, with Lowe's being worse for online stock listings. Despite generally defaulting to Home Depot for smarter staff and better budget tools, he recommends Lowe's for this specific purchase.
quotes.key_insight: Even loyal Home Depot customers should comparison-shop on consumables like sandpaper, where Lowe's can offer double the quantity of a better brand at the same price.

He visits multiple Seattle lumber retailers, finding each lacking in different ways — limited stock, poor quality, high prices, or unhelpful staff. He concludes that retail wood buying is a poor experience and recommends finding a good sawyer and building a direct relationship with them.
quotes.key_insight: For quality woodworking materials, direct relationships with sawyers beat retail lumber yards on price, selection, and quality.

quotes.quote: The subject approaches the ever-enlightening 'linux versus windows' debates for elucidating online reading.
quotes.summary: Shapiro recounts his attempt to build an end-grain butcher block cutting board from oak, only to discover that oak's extremely open pores make it unsuitable for food preparation — shellac soaked straight through the 1.75-inch board. He improvises a fix using diluted wood glue and powdered pumice to fill the pores, then sands it smooth. The post is a lighthearted woodworking cautionary tale that ties into the broader online debate about wood versus plastic cutting boards and food safety. His conclusion: just use maple.
quotes.key_insight: Choosing the right material matters more than clever fixes — oak's open pores make it fundamentally wrong for cutting boards, and no amount of improvisation fully substitutes for starting with the right wood.

quotes.quote: I'm sorry to say, Ridgid tools are now, officially, crappy.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro declares that Ridgid power tools have crossed from the 'Good and Expensive' category to 'Expensive and Crappy.' He notes that Ridgid is moving manufacturing overseas, cutting their warranty from lifetime to two years, expanding retail distribution beyond Home Depot, and maintaining premium prices despite the quality decline. He advises readers to avoid Ridgid and buy competitors like DeWalt instead.
quotes.key_insight: When a brand cuts quality while keeping premium prices, it creates the worst possible market position—expensive and crappy—and consumers should abandon it immediately.

quotes.quote: One of the good things about being a woodworker is that you can whip out great gifts with very little work.
quotes.summary: Shapiro shares a simple woodworking project: handmade children's building blocks from scrap hardwood. He walks through the quick process of gluing, cutting, sanding, and finishing blocks from maple and walnut scraps. The post frames the project as an ideal gift for baby showers—something that looks impressive and can't be bought in stores, yet requires minimal effort from anyone with basic shop tools. It's a practical maker tip wrapped in the satisfaction of turning scraps into heirlooms.
quotes.key_insight: The best handmade gifts maximize the gap between perceived value and actual effort—simple woodworking techniques turn shop scraps into heirloom-quality children's toys.

quotes.quote: There are two kinds of engineering in this world. One kind takes place at a desk with a pencil and paper. The other takes place when you walk into Home Depot and jump on a wheelbarrow to determine if it can move the three-man rock in your yard without collapsing.
quotes.summary: Shapiro contrasts two kinds of engineering: theoretical calculation and hands-on physical intuition. He illustrates this with stories of bouncing on metal beams to test their strength for a motorized couch, and a classmate who tried to design a grass-cutting device using string on a hand-cranked pole without recognizing the obvious physical impracticality. The post argues that real engineering competence requires getting your hands dirty and testing materials physically, not just running calculations. He closes with a professor's wisdom that the best engineers learn by breaking things deliberately.
quotes.key_insight: Physical intuition and hands-on experimentation are as essential to good engineering as theoretical calculation — don't be the person who can solve the equations but can't tell that string on a hand crank won't cut grass.

quotes.quote: It seemed ballpeen-hammer-to-the-forehead-crease obvious to me that you couldn't cut grass by waving string at it, but not so Ziggy.

quotes.quote: Don't search for planers. Search for planrs, plainers, playners… have a couple of beers and let your mind roam free.
quotes.summary: Shapiro reveals a clever eBay shopping hack: searching for misspelled product names to find underpriced listings. He demonstrates that many eBay sellers can't spell their products correctly, resulting in fewer bidders and lower prices compared to correctly-listed items or Amazon. The post uses woodworking tools as examples, showing that a planer on eBay with correct spelling costs nearly as much as Amazon after shipping. The core economic insight is that efficient markets work against bargain hunters, so introducing inefficiency through misspelled searches creates opportunity.
quotes.key_insight: Market inefficiencies created by seller misspellings on eBay are exploitable arbitrage opportunities for savvy buyers.

quotes.quote: It turns out that many ebay sellers are spelling-impaired, and their confusion is your gain.

quotes.quote: Do you think Darth Vader would put up with an itchy, sweaty, ineffective piece of fiberglass rubbing against his nose all day? Neither should you.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro humorously recommends upgrading from a standard fiberglass dust mask to a more comfortable respirator-style mask. He uses Darth Vader as a comedic framing device, arguing that the traditional dust mask is itchy, sweaty, and ineffective. The recommended alternative is more comfortable, works better, and is affordable. The post is a lighthearted product recommendation from Shapiro's early blogging days.
quotes.key_insight: A small equipment upgrade — swapping a cheap dust mask for a proper respirator — can dramatically improve comfort and effectiveness for makers and builders.

quotes.quote: It is crazy comfortable, it works way better, it's cheap, and you won't be blowing strange-looking black things into the kleenex all day.

quotes.quote: You can turn the dust from your Random Orbital Sander of Eternal Happiness into instant liquid wood.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a DIY woodworking tip inspired by a wedding cake emergency. When his sister-in-law needed a pastry bag the day before his wedding, she improvised with Ziploc bags to frost the cake. Shapiro applies the same principle to woodworking: mixing sawdust from sanding with wood glue in a Ziploc bag creates 'Instant Liquid Wood' that can fill gaps and take stain like the surrounding material. The post is a lighthearted, early-blog-era maker tip that exemplifies Shapiro's tinkerer spirit.
quotes.key_insight: Creative cross-pollination between domains — borrowing a baking improvisation for woodworking — is the essence of maker ingenuity.

quotes.quote: Just add about 3 parts Titebond (or other caesin glue) to one part sawdust and mix it all up in the bag.

quotes.quote: Take it off, hang it on the wall, and save it for when your friend comes over to borrow the table saw.
quotes.summary: Shapiro makes a straightforward case for upgrading your tablesaw blade, arguing that a quality 80-tooth blade is the single best improvement you can make to a table saw. He contends that blade quality matters more than the saw itself, noting that a cheap saw with a good blade outperforms an expensive saw with a bad one. The post advises keeping your old low-tooth blade for rough lumber work and using the new blade for hardwoods. It's a short, practical maker tip delivered with Shapiro's characteristic humor.
quotes.key_insight: The quality of your blade matters far more than the quality of your saw — upgrading a single consumable component transforms the output of the entire tool.

quotes.quote: A $400 table saw with a new 80-tooth blade cuts better than an $800 table saw with a crappy low-tooth-count blade.

quotes.quote: The Random Orbital Sander is the single best-bang-for-the-buck powertool out there.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro makes an enthusiastic case for the random orbital sander as the best value power tool available. He provides a concise buying guide with must-have features (8 holes, velcro sandpaper, 5-inch minimum, at least $60) and dismisses unnecessary extras like variable speed or carrying cases. The post explains the tool's versatility—from aggressive material removal with coarse grit to mirror-smooth finishing—all without the complexity of belt sanders. It's an early example of Shapiro's maker culture voice, blending practical advice with humor.
quotes.key_insight: The random orbital sander is the one power tool that punches far above its price point, handling nearly all sanding tasks without the learning curve or complexity of specialized alternatives.

quotes.quote: I'm learning lots of lessons, some the easy way, some with liberal applications of neosporin.
quotes.summary: Dan Shapiro introduces his woodworking blog with a brief, humorous post explaining that he started serious woodworking about a year prior. He promises to share lessons learned, acknowledging that some have come painlessly while others involved minor injuries. The post's title 'Severed?' is a self-deprecating joke about the risks of power tools.
quotes.key_insight: This brief introductory post establishes Shapiro's maker identity and self-deprecating humor style that would characterize his later writing.
